searchDocs.title: The Layered Architecture​
searchDocs.content: Modern spacecraft FSW follows a layered architecture:  ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Application Layer │ │ Mission sequences, autonomy rules, │ │ science instrument control │ ├──────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Services Layer │ │ Command & telemetry, event logging, │ │ file management, time services │ ├──────────────────────────────────────┤ │ OSAL / PSP │ │ OS Abstraction Layer, │ │ Platform Support Package │ ├──────────────────────────────────────┤ │ RTOS │ │ FreeRTOS, RTEMS, VxWorks │ ├──────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Hardware │ │ OBC, sensors, actuators, buses │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘   The layering is not academic — it directly enables two critical properties:  Portability: The application layer can run on different hardware (different OBC, different RTOS) by only changing the OSAL/PSP layerTestability: Application components can be tested on desktop without real hardware

searchDocs.title: NASA's Core Flight System (cFS)​
searchDocs.content: cFS is NASA's open-source reusable flight software framework. It defines a component-based architecture where software applications ("apps") communicate through a message bus (the Software Bus, SB). Each app has a specific responsibility and no direct dependencies on other apps.  cFS is used on the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter, LCRD, PACE, and dozens of CubeSats and commercial missions. If you're going into flight software, understanding cFS is nearly mandatory.  See cFS Framework for the full deep dive.

searchDocs.title: The Three Loops​
searchDocs.content: Flight software is structured around three time scales:  Fast loop (1–100 Hz): Attitude control, sensor reading, actuator commanding. Must be deterministic. Runs in highest-priority RTOS task.  Medium loop (1–10 Hz): Telemetry collection, health monitoring, mode management. Responds to state changes.  Slow loop (0.1–1 Hz): Ground command processing, data storage, non-critical diagnostics. Tolerates some latency.

searchDocs.title: The Core Insight​
searchDocs.content: Space software engineering is not harder than other software engineering. It is different — and the differences come from physics, not from convention.  The machine you're targeting:  Has 256 KB of RAM and runs at 25 MHzOperates at −40°C to +85°C with no air for coolingGets hit by ~50 high-energy particle strikes per dayCannot be rebooted by a human if it locks upHas a 12-minute communication window every 94 minutesMust execute deterministically — a missed deadline is a mission failure  Every pattern in this reference exists because of those constraints. malloc is banned not because it's slow, but because it's non-deterministic — if heap fragmentation causes an allocation failure at the wrong moment, the spacecraft may not recover. Exceptions are disabled not as style preference, but because unhandled exceptions on a spacecraft are unrecoverable. Triple modular redundancy isn't paranoia — it's the minimum viable fault tolerance for hardware that experiences radiation-induced bit flips.  Once you internalize the constraints, the code starts to make sense.

searchDocs.title: How to Use This​
searchDocs.content: The reference is divided into six disciplines that build on each other:  Section	What It TeachesEmbedded C/C++	The language patterns that work in constrained, safety-critical environments RTOS	How real-time schedulers work and how to use them correctly Space Hardware	The physical hardware stack your code runs on Flight Software	How real spacecraft software is architectured and operated Orbital Physics	The math your software implements — orbits, attitude, estimation Safety & Reliability	How you prove the software is correct before it launches  The Synthesis section is where all six come together: each page takes a real spacecraft software component and shows exactly how the disciplines interact in a production implementation.  You can read sections independently based on your gaps. If you understand embedded C but have never touched an RTOS, start with RTOS. If you're strong on the software side but don't know what SGP4 is, start with Orbital Physics.

searchDocs.title: Who This Is For​
searchDocs.content: Software engineers preparing for flight software roles at companies like Firefly, Relativity Space, Spire, Planet Labs, Rocket Lab, or similarEmbedded engineers transitioning from automotive or industrial systems to spaceSystems engineers who want to understand the software they're specifyingHobbyists building CubeSat or amateur satellite projects who want to understand how the professionals do it  Strong C/C++ fundamentals assumed. No aerospace background required. No prior embedded experience required, though it helps.

searchDocs.title: The Mindset Shift​
searchDocs.content: Most software engineers are trained to think about correctness and performance. Space software engineers think about correctness, determinism, and fault recovery — in that order.  Desktop software: "Does it produce the right output?" Space software: "Does it always produce the right output, within a bounded time, even after a radiation-induced bit flip, even if the ground station is out of range for 6 hours?"   The second question is structurally harder. It requires different language patterns, different architecture, and different verification methods. This reference teaches all three.

searchDocs.title: A Note on Standards​
searchDocs.content: You will encounter several standards throughout this reference:  MISRA-C:2012 — C coding standard for safety-critical embedded systemsDO-178C — Software certification standard for avionics (also used in space)ECSS-E-ST-40 — ESA's space software engineering standardCCSDS — Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems — packet and protocol standards used by NASA, ESA, and most space agencies  These are not bureaucratic obstacles. They encode decades of failure analysis — every rule exists because something went wrong without it. Understanding them makes you a better engineer whether or not you're seeking certification.

searchDocs.title: What Changes​
searchDocs.content: Assumption	Desktop	Space EmbeddedMemory	Virtually unlimited	64 KB – 8 MB RAM, static allocation only Heap	new/malloc freely used	Banned in flight code — non-deterministic Exceptions	Try/catch is idiomatic	Disabled — unhandled exception = unrecoverable Dynamic dispatch	virtual everywhere	Avoided in critical paths — vtable indirection has cost OS	Full preemptive OS	Bare metal or minimal RTOS Hardware access	Through drivers, abstracted	Direct register access via volatile MMIO Startup	main() just starts	Startup code initializes stack, BSS, peripheral clocks Failure	Process crashes, is restarted	Watchdog fires, system resets — must survive and recover

searchDocs.title: No dynamic allocation in flight code​
searchDocs.content: // This is forbidden in flight software uint8_t* buf = malloc(256); // This is how you do it static uint8_t buf[256]; // Allocated at link time, size known statically   Why: malloc can fail due to heap fragmentation. On a spacecraft, an allocation failure at the wrong moment has no recovery path. Static allocation means memory usage is 100% determined at link time — you can prove at build time that the system has enough RAM.

searchDocs.title: volatile is not optional​
searchDocs.content: Every memory-mapped hardware register must be declared volatile. Without it, the compiler is free to optimize away reads and writes it considers redundant:  // WRONG: compiler may optimize the polling loop away uint32_t* status_reg = (uint32_t*)0x40001000; while (!(*status_reg & 0x01)); // wait for ready bit // CORRECT volatile uint32_t* status_reg = (volatile uint32_t*)0x40001000; while (!(*status_reg & 0x01)); // compiler must re-read each iteration

searchDocs.title: Deterministic timing is a hard requirement​
searchDocs.content: Flight software has deadlines. A sensor read that takes 5 ms on average but occasionally takes 50 ms (due to cache misses, malloc, or OS scheduling jitter) is not acceptable in a hard real-time system. Every function on the critical path must have a bounded worst-case execution time (WCET).

searchDocs.title: The Startup Sequence​
searchDocs.content: When a microcontroller powers on or resets, the hardware does a minimal set of things before your code runs:  PC (program counter) is loaded from the reset vectorStack pointer is loaded from the vector tableExecution jumps to the reset handler  Everything else — clock initialization, peripheral setup, BSS clearing, data segment copying — is your responsibility. The reset handler, usually written in assembly or low-level C, handles this before calling main().  Typical startup sequence:  // startup.c — what happens before main() extern uint32_t _sdata; // start of .data in RAM (linked symbol) extern uint32_t _edata; // end of .data in RAM extern uint32_t _sidata; // source of .data in FLASH extern uint32_t _sbss; // start of .bss in RAM extern uint32_t _ebss; // end of .bss in RAM void Reset_Handler(void) { // 1. Copy .data section from FLASH to RAM uint32_t *src = &_sidata; uint32_t *dst = &_sdata; while (dst < &_edata) { *dst++ = *src++; } // 2. Zero-initialize .bss section dst = &_sbss; while (dst < &_ebss) { *dst++ = 0U; } // 3. Initialize system clocks, PLL, flash wait states SystemInit(); // 4. Call main main(); // 5. If main returns (it shouldn't), loop forever while (1); }

searchDocs.title: The Linker Script​
searchDocs.content: The linker script defines the memory map of your target: where FLASH is, where RAM is, and how the different sections of your binary get placed into them.  /* linker.ld — STM32F4 example */ MEMORY { FLASH (rx) : ORIGIN = 0x08000000, LENGTH = 1024K RAM (xrw) : ORIGIN = 0x20000000, LENGTH = 192K } SECTIONS { /* Vector table + code + const data → FLASH */ .text : { KEEP(*(.isr_vector)) /* vector table must be first */ *(.text*) *(.rodata*) } > FLASH /* .data initial values stored in FLASH, copied to RAM at startup */ .data : { _sdata = .; *(.data*) _edata = .; } > RAM AT > FLASH _sidata = LOADADDR(.data); /* .bss is zero-initialized RAM — no FLASH storage needed */ .bss : { _sbss = .; *(.bss*) *(COMMON) _ebss = .; } > RAM /* Stack placed at top of RAM, grows downward */ ._stack (NOLOAD) : { . = ALIGN(8); _estack = ORIGIN(RAM) + LENGTH(RAM); } > RAM }   Key concepts:  .text — code and read-only data → stored in FLASH, executed in place.data — initialized globals → stored in FLASH, copied to RAM at startup.bss — zero-initialized globals → RAM only, zeroed by startup code.stack — stack space → RAM, grows down from top

searchDocs.title: The Vector Table​
searchDocs.content: On ARM Cortex-M, the vector table is an array of function pointers placed at address 0x00000000 (or 0x08000000 for STM32, remapped by the boot ROM). The first two entries are the initial stack pointer and the reset handler.  // vector_table.c extern void Reset_Handler(void); extern void NMI_Handler(void); extern void HardFault_Handler(void); // ... more fault handlers ... extern void SysTick_Handler(void); extern void UART1_IRQHandler(void); __attribute__((section(".isr_vector"))) const uint32_t vector_table[] = { (uint32_t)&_estack, // [0] initial stack pointer (uint32_t)&Reset_Handler, // [1] reset handler (uint32_t)&NMI_Handler, // [2] NMI (uint32_t)&HardFault_Handler, // [3] hard fault 0, 0, 0, 0, // [4-7] reserved 0, 0, 0, // [8-10] reserved (uint32_t)&SysTick_Handler, // [15] SysTick (uint32_t)&UART1_IRQHandler, // [16] UART1 interrupt // ... more peripheral IRQs ... };

searchDocs.title: Memory-Mapped I/O​
searchDocs.content: On bare metal, you control hardware by reading and writing specific memory addresses. These are defined in the chip's reference manual.  // Blink an LED on GPIOA pin 5 (STM32F4) // Base addresses from reference manual #define RCC_BASE 0x40023800UL #define GPIOA_BASE 0x40020000UL // Register offsets #define RCC_AHB1ENR (*(volatile uint32_t*)(RCC_BASE + 0x30)) #define GPIOA_MODER (*(volatile uint32_t*)(GPIOA_BASE + 0x00)) #define GPIOA_ODR (*(volatile uint32_t*)(GPIOA_BASE + 0x14)) void init_led(void) { // Enable GPIOA clock in RCC RCC_AHB1ENR |= (1U << 0); // Configure PA5 as output (MODER bits [11:10] = 01) GPIOA_MODER &= ~(3U << 10); // clear GPIOA_MODER |= (1U << 10); // set to output } void toggle_led(void) { GPIOA_ODR ^= (1U << 5); }   Every register access uses volatile — without it, the compiler may eliminate the write as "dead code" since the variable is never read back in the same function.

searchDocs.title: The Main Loop​
searchDocs.content: Bare metal embedded software is typically structured as an infinite loop. There is no OS to schedule tasks — you schedule them yourself.  int main(void) { // Hardware initialization SystemClock_Config(); // PLL, clock sources GPIO_Init(); UART_Init(); SPI_Init(); Watchdog_Init(); // Main superloop — runs forever while (1) { // 1 ms tick from SysTick interrupt sets this flag if (tick_1ms_flag) { tick_1ms_flag = 0; run_1ms_tasks(); } // Less frequent tasks if (tick_10ms_flag) { tick_10ms_flag = 0; run_10ms_tasks(); } if (tick_100ms_flag) { tick_100ms_flag = 0; run_100ms_tasks(); } // Kick the watchdog — if this stops, the system resets Watchdog_Kick(); } }   This is the superloop pattern. It is simple, deterministic, and analyzable. For more complex scheduling requirements, see RTOS.

searchDocs.title: Hard Fault Handler​
searchDocs.content: When something goes catastrophically wrong (null pointer dereference, stack overflow, illegal instruction), the ARM Cortex-M processor fires a HardFault exception. In flight software, you must handle this — and ideally log diagnostic information before resetting.  void HardFault_Handler(void) { // Capture the fault context from the stack // ARM pushes PC, LR, xPSR, R0-R3, R12 on exception entry // Log to non-volatile storage if possible fault_log_write(FAULT_HARD_FAULT, __get_MSP(), __get_PSP()); // Increment fault counter in persistent RAM (survives warm reset) fault_count++; // Reset NVIC_SystemReset(); }   In production flight software, the hard fault handler is one of the most important functions. It is your last chance to record what went wrong before the watchdog or deliberate reset wipes the volatile state.

searchDocs.title: Why MISRA-C Exists​
searchDocs.content: C is powerful precisely because it gives you low-level access to hardware and makes few guarantees about behavior. The same features that make C fast and flexible — pointer arithmetic, implicit type conversions, undefined behavior — are also the features that cause subtle, hard-to-reproduce bugs in safety-critical systems.  MISRA-C restricts the language to a well-defined, analyzable subset. Every rule has a documented rationale, usually rooted in a specific class of real bugs or undefined behavior.

searchDocs.title: Rule Categories​
searchDocs.content: Category	What It MeansMandatory	Must always be complied with. No deviation permitted. Required	Must be complied with unless a formal deviation is documented and approved Advisory	Should be complied with; deviation requires less formal justification

searchDocs.title: Rule 1.3 — No undefined behavior​
searchDocs.content: Undefined behavior in C is not just "the result is unpredictable." It means the compiler is free to assume it never happens, and will optimize accordingly — often in ways that silently break your code.  // UB: signed integer overflow int x = INT_MAX; int y = x + 1; // compiler may assume this never happens → optimizes out checks // UB: reading uninitialized variable int val; if (val > 0) { ... } // val contains garbage // UB: null pointer dereference int* p = NULL; *p = 5;

searchDocs.title: Rule 8.7 — Functions with external linkage need declarations​
searchDocs.content: Every function accessible from outside its translation unit must be declared in a header. This prevents implicit declarations and type mismatches between caller and callee.

searchDocs.title: Rule 10.x — Essentially typed conversions​
searchDocs.content: MISRA-C has a detailed type system called "essential types" that tracks whether values are signed, unsigned, Boolean, character, etc. Implicit conversions between essential types are forbidden — you must make them explicit and intentional.  // Violation: implicit conversion from signed to unsigned int16_t signed_val = -1; uint32_t result = signed_val; // wraps to 0xFFFFFFFF — probably not intended // Compliant: explicit, intentional cast uint32_t result = (uint32_t)(uint16_t)signed_val; // clearly intentional

searchDocs.title: Rule 11.x — Pointer conversions​
searchDocs.content: Pointer casts are heavily restricted. Converting between pointer types or between pointers and integers must be explicit and is often flagged for review.  // MISRA violation: cast from integer to pointer (required for MMIO) volatile uint32_t* reg = (volatile uint32_t*)0x40001000; // → Must be documented as a permitted deviation for hardware access

searchDocs.title: Rule 14.4 — The controlling expression of if must be essentially Boolean​
searchDocs.content: // Violation: integer used as Boolean int flag = get_status(); if (flag) { ... } // flag is int, not bool — violation // Compliant bool flag = (get_status() != 0); if (flag) { ... }

searchDocs.title: Rule 15.5 — A function shall have a single point of exit​
searchDocs.content: // Violation: multiple return points int process(int x) { if (x < 0) return -1; // early return — violation do_work(x); return 0; } // Compliant: single exit point int process(int x) { int result = 0; if (x < 0) { result = -1; } else { do_work(x); } return result; }   This rule is controversial. It forces structured code but can make some functions harder to read. Many teams take a formal deviation for guard clauses.

searchDocs.title: Rule 17.2 — No recursion​
searchDocs.content: Functions shall not call themselves directly or indirectly. Recursion makes stack depth unbounded and unanalyzable.  // MISRA violation int factorial(int n) { if (n <= 1) return 1; return n * factorial(n - 1); // recursion — banned } // Compliant: iterative int factorial(int n) { int result = 1; for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) { result *= i; } return result; }

searchDocs.title: Rule 21.x — Standard library restrictions​
searchDocs.content: Several standard library functions are restricted or banned:  malloc, calloc, realloc, free — banned (dynamic memory)printf, scanf — restricted (format string vulnerabilities, non-deterministic)setjmp, longjmp — banned (bypasses normal call stack)system() — banned

searchDocs.title: Static Analysis Tools​
searchDocs.content: MISRA-C compliance is verified with static analysis tools, not the compiler:  Tool	NotesPC-lint Plus	Comprehensive, commercial, widely used in safety-critical Polyspace (MathWorks)	Deep static analysis, used in automotive and aerospace Coverity	Commercial, also catches security vulnerabilities Cppcheck	Open source, partial MISRA support LDRA	Widely used in DO-178C avionics certification  Running the compiler alone is not sufficient. MISRA violations are often valid C that compiles without warnings.

searchDocs.title: MISRA vs. C++ (AUTOSAR, HIC++)​
searchDocs.content: MISRA-C:2012 covers C. For C++, the equivalent standards are:  MISRA-C++:2008 — older, widely usedAUTOSAR C++14 — automotive, more modern, based on C++14HIC++ v4.0 — High Integrity C++, used in defense and aerospace  The C++ standards follow the same philosophy: restrict the language to a deterministic, analyzable subset. Key additions for C++:  No exceptions (-fno-exceptions)No RTTI (-fno-rtti)virtual functions restricted or banned in critical pathsNo dynamic_castTemplates must not increase binary size unexpectedly

searchDocs.title: Practical Approach​
searchDocs.content: In practice, most flight software teams:  Enable MISRA-C checking in their CI pipelineFix all mandatory violations — no exceptionsFix required violations or document formal deviationsApply advisory rules by judgment, especially for hardware access codeMaintain a deviation log that is reviewed before each release  Perfect MISRA compliance is rarely the goal. The goal is using the standard as a tool to catch the specific classes of bugs — type confusion, undefined behavior, unanalyzable control flow — that cause silent failures in safety-critical systems.

searchDocs.title: Why No malloc​
searchDocs.content: malloc can fail due to heap fragmentation. On a spacecraft, a failed allocation at a critical moment has no safe recovery path. Static allocation means:  Memory use is 100% determinable at build timeNo runtime allocation failuresNo fragmentation — everSimpler MISRA-C compliance (Rule 21.3 forbids dynamic memory)

searchDocs.title: Static Allocation Patterns​
searchDocs.content: // Global static buffer — allocated at link time in .bss static uint8_t telemetry_buffer[4096]; // Stack-allocated — function scope only, within stack budget void process_packet(void) { uint8_t local_buf[128]; // lives on stack for duration of function // ... } // Static local — persists across calls, not on heap void sensor_accumulate(int16_t sample) { static int32_t accumulator = 0; static uint32_t count = 0; accumulator += sample; count++; }

searchDocs.title: Memory Pool​
searchDocs.content: When you need "dynamic-like" behavior (varying number of objects), use a pre-allocated pool:  #define POOL_SIZE 16 #define OBJ_SIZE sizeof(Command_t) static uint8_t pool_mem[POOL_SIZE][OBJ_SIZE]; static bool pool_used[POOL_SIZE] = {false}; void* pool_alloc(void) { for (int i = 0; i < POOL_SIZE; i++) { if (!pool_used[i]) { pool_used[i] = true; return pool_mem[i]; } } return NULL; // pool exhausted — handle at call site } void pool_free(void* ptr) { for (int i = 0; i < POOL_SIZE; i++) { if ((void*)pool_mem[i] == ptr) { pool_used[i] = false; return; } } }

searchDocs.title: Stack Sizing​
searchDocs.content: Each RTOS task needs its own stack. Size it by:  Enabling stack overflow detection (FreeRTOS Method 2)Running worst-case scenarios in testChecking uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark() — the number of unused wordsAdding 30% margin above measured worst-case  // Check stack headroom during development UBaseType_t high_water = uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark(adcs_task_handle); // high_water is the minimum number of words that were ever free // If this is less than ~32 words, increase the stack

searchDocs.title: Why This Matters​
searchDocs.content: A spacecraft is a physical object governed by Newton's laws, Einstein's corrections, and the geometry of the solar system. The software must model and respond to this physical reality.  Errors have direct consequences:  Wrong quaternion convention → attitude estimate is off by 180° → solar panels point away from the sunWrong time system → GPS position error of meters to kilometers depending on how you compute itEuler angle singularity (gimbal lock) → attitude controller fails at a specific orientationOrbit propagation error → predicted ground contact windows are wrong → telemetry is not downlinkedWrong coordinate frame → thruster fires in the wrong direction  These are not hypothetical. They have happened on real missions.

searchDocs.title: The Coordinate Frames​
searchDocs.content: Everything in orbital mechanics requires you to specify which frame a vector is expressed in. Confusion between frames is the most common source of errors.  Frame	Origin	Definition	Use CaseECI (Earth Centered Inertial)	Earth center	X toward vernal equinox, Z toward north pole, non-rotating	Orbit propagation, absolute position ECEF (Earth Centered Earth Fixed)	Earth center	Rotates with Earth — X toward prime meridian	GPS, ground station location LVLH (Local Vertical Local Horizontal)	Spacecraft	Z toward Earth center, X along velocity vector	Relative navigation, formation flying Body	Spacecraft center	Fixed to spacecraft structure	Attitude control, sensor mounting Orbit	Spacecraft	Z along orbital angular momentum	Orbital maneuvers

searchDocs.title: NASA Open Source​
searchDocs.content: Core Flight System (cFS)github.com/nasa/cFSThe framework used on dozens of NASA missions. Read the architecture document and the cFE user's guide before writing any flight software.  OSAL (OS Abstraction Layer)github.com/nasa/osalStandalone RTOS abstraction library. Run the same code on FreeRTOS, RTEMS, and Linux.  F' (F Prime)github.com/nasa/fprimeJPL's component-based flight software framework. Used on Mars Ingenuity helicopter. C++, component-based, ground-testable.  NASA Software Engineering Handbook (NPR 7150.2)NASA Technical StandardsThe standard that governs NASA software development. Defines software classification (Class A–D), required artifacts, and process requirements.  ITAR Note: Some NASA documents are export-controlled. Check before distributing.

searchDocs.title: Standards (Free Access)​
searchDocs.content: CCSDS Standardspublic.ccsds.org/Publications/BlueBooks.aspxAll CCSDS standards are freely available. Essential reading:  CCSDS 133.0-B-2: Space Packet Protocol (the packet format used in cFS)CCSDS 232.0-B-4: TC Space Data Link Protocol (command uplink)CCSDS 131.0-B-3: TM Synchronization and Channel Coding  MISRA C:2012 — Available for purchase from MISRA consortium (~£30 GBP).  DO-178C — RTCA/DO-178C, available from RTCA (~$300). Expensive but necessary if pursuing certification work.

searchDocs.title: Books​
searchDocs.content: "Real-Time Systems Design and Analysis" — Phillip Laplante Scheduling theory, WCET analysis, RTOS concepts. Dense but authoritative.  "Spacecraft Dynamics and Control" — Marcel Sidi ADCS system design, control laws, sensors, actuators. One of the better textbooks for implementers.  "Fundamentals of Spacecraft Attitude Determination and Control" — Markley & Crassidis The attitude determination reference. Quaternions, EKF, TRIAD, QUEST. Essential for anyone implementing ADCS algorithms.  "Satellite Communications Systems Engineering" — Pratt & Bostian Link budgets, propagation, modulation. Good for understanding what the communications team is calculating.  "Embedded Systems Design" — Steve Heath Practical embedded hardware/software co-design. Good grounding for the hardware-software interface.  "Software Engineering for Embedded and Real-Time Systems" — Roland Bbuttonell Practical. Covers MISRA-C, WCET, testing strategies for embedded.

searchDocs.title: RTOS Documentation​
searchDocs.content: FreeRTOSfreertos.org/DocumentationFree PDF. Comprehensive. Read the mastering FreeRTOS book — it covers internals, not just API.  RTEMSdocs.rtems.orgComplete documentation including POSIX API, BSP development guide, and application notes.  Zephyrdocs.zephyrproject.orgThorough documentation for the kernel, device drivers, and subsystems.

searchDocs.title: Orbital Mechanics​
searchDocs.content: David Vallado — "Fundamentals of Astrodynamics and Applications"The standard reference for orbit propagation, coordinate transforms, and time systems. SGP4 reference implementation by Vallado is the authoritative implementation.  Celestrak — celestrak.orgTLE data, SGP4 reference code, atmospheric density models.  GMAT (General Mission Analysis Tool)software.nasa.gov/software/GSC-17177-1NASA's free mission analysis tool. Use to validate orbit propagator implementations.

searchDocs.title: Radiation & Reliability​
searchDocs.content: ESA ESCC/ECSS Standardsescies.orgEuropean component and process standards. ECSS-Q-ST-60-15C covers radiation hardness assurance.  NASA GSFC Radiation Effects & Analysis Groupradhome.gsfc.nasa.govTest data for radiation effects on components.  JPL Radiation Handbookradcentral.jpl.nasa.govComprehensive reference for radiation effects in space systems.

searchDocs.title: Communities​
searchDocs.content: Space Stack Exchangespace.stackexchange.comGood for specific technical questions on orbital mechanics, mission design.  r/aerospace and r/spaceflightLess technical but useful for staying current on industry news.  AIAA (American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics)aiaa.orgTechnical papers on FSW, GNC, systems engineering. Student membership is affordable.  SmallSat Conference (Logan, Utah)Annual conference focused on CubeSats and small satellites. Good for understanding commercial small sat FSW trends.

searchDocs.title: Relationship Between Standards​
searchDocs.content: DO-178C ←── Used by NASA missions voluntarily (especially for DAL A/B) ↑ │ ECSS-E-ST-40 ←── ESA-funded missions (Sentinel, BepiColombo, JUICE) ↑ │ NPR 7150.2 ←── All NASA programs (Class A = highest, D = lowest rigor)   For commercial small satellites, most companies apply MISRA-C and internal coding standards without formal DO-178C certification. Certification becomes required when the spacecraft is safety-of-life (crew, life support) or when selling to a customer that requires it (e.g., FAA-regulated launch).

searchDocs.title: A​
searchDocs.content: ADCS — Attitude Determination and Control System. The subsystem responsible for determining the spacecraft's orientation and commanding actuators to achieve a desired attitude.  AGC — Automatic Gain Control. Receiver circuit that adjusts signal amplification based on incoming signal strength.  AIT — Assembly, Integration, and Test. The process of assembling spacecraft components, integrating subsystems, and testing the complete system.  AOS — Acquisition of Signal. The moment a ground station establishes communication with a spacecraft.  AOCS — Attitude and Orbit Control System. European term for what Americans call ADCS, sometimes extended to include orbit maneuver capability.

searchDocs.title: B​
searchDocs.content: Bdot — Rate-based detumble control law. Applies magnetic torque proportional to the rate of change of the measured magnetic field. Simple, reliable, requires only a magnetometer and magnetorquers. Used as initial detumble mode after deployment.  BOL — Beginning of Life. Performance specifications at time of launch, before any degradation.  BSS — Block Started by Symbol. Memory section for zero-initialized global and static variables.

searchDocs.title: C​
searchDocs.content: CCSDS — Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems. International organization that defines space communication standards. CCSDS packets are the standard format for spacecraft command and telemetry.  CDH — Command and Data Handling. The subsystem responsible for processing commands and managing onboard data.  CEI — Contract End Item. A deliverable defined in a government contract.  CMG — Control Moment Gyroscope. High-torque attitude actuator used on large spacecraft (ISS). Distinct from reaction wheels.  CoG / CoM — Center of Gravity / Center of Mass.

searchDocs.title: D​
searchDocs.content: DAL — Development Assurance Level. DO-178C classification (A–E) indicating required rigor of software development based on severity of failure consequence.  DRAM — Dynamic RAM. Volatile memory requiring periodic refresh. More susceptible to SEUs than SRAM. Rarely used in space for critical data.  DSN — Deep Space Network. NASA's network of large ground antennas supporting interplanetary missions.

searchDocs.title: E​
searchDocs.content: ECSS — European Cooperation for Space Standardization. Body that produces space standards including ECSS-E-ST-40 for software.  EDAC — Error Detection and Correction. Hardware implementation of ECC for memory. Often used interchangeably with ECC.  ECI — Earth Centered Inertial. Coordinate frame with origin at Earth's center, non-rotating, X toward vernal equinox.  ECEF — Earth Centered Earth Fixed. Rotating with Earth. Used for GPS and ground station coordinates.  EKF — Extended Kalman Filter. Nonlinear extension of the Kalman filter, used for attitude and orbit estimation.  EOL — End of Life. Performance specifications at end of mission.  EPS — Electrical Power System. Subsystem managing solar panels, batteries, and power distribution.

searchDocs.title: F​
searchDocs.content: FDIR — Fault Detection, Isolation, and Recovery. The autonomous fault management system.  FEC — Forward Error Correction. Error correction codes applied to transmitted data so the receiver can correct errors without retransmission.  FOV — Field of View.  FSW / FSW — Flight Software.

searchDocs.title: G​
searchDocs.content: GCR — Galactic Cosmic Ray. High-energy particles from outside the solar system.  GEO — Geostationary Earth Orbit. ~35,786 km altitude, orbital period = 24 hours, appears stationary from ground.  GOES — Geostationary Operational Environmental Satellite. NOAA weather satellites.  GPS — Global Positioning System. Also refers to the time system: GPS time = TAI − 19 seconds, does not include leap seconds.

searchDocs.title: H​
searchDocs.content: HGA — High Gain Antenna. Directional antenna providing high data rate but narrow beam — must be pointed.  HIL / HITL — Hardware-in-the-Loop testing. Test configuration where real hardware is included in the test loop.  HSM — Hierarchical State Machine. State machine where states can have sub-states, with entry/exit/transition actions.  HK / Housekeeping — Periodic telemetry of spacecraft health data (temperatures, voltages, currents, mode).

searchDocs.title: I​
searchDocs.content: IMU — Inertial Measurement Unit. Sensor combining gyroscopes and accelerometers.  IPC — Inter-Process Communication. Mechanisms for tasks/processes to share data (queues, semaphores, shared memory).  ISL — Inter-Satellite Link. Communication link between satellites in a constellation.  ISR — Interrupt Service Routine. Code that executes in response to a hardware interrupt.

searchDocs.title: K​
searchDocs.content: Keplerian Elements — Six orbital elements that uniquely define an orbit: semi-major axis (a), eccentricity (e), inclination (i), RAAN (Ω), argument of periapsis (ω), true anomaly (ν).

searchDocs.title: L​
searchDocs.content: LEO — Low Earth Orbit. Roughly 200–2,000 km altitude.  LOS — Loss of Signal. When a ground station loses contact with a spacecraft.  LV — Launch Vehicle.  LVLH — Local Vertical Local Horizontal. Spacecraft-centered coordinate frame where Z points toward Earth center, X along velocity vector.

searchDocs.title: M​
searchDocs.content: MEO — Medium Earth Orbit. 2,000–35,786 km.  MET — Mission Elapsed Time. Time since spacecraft separation from launch vehicle.  MMIO — Memory-Mapped I/O. Hardware registers addressed as memory locations.  MTQ / MTR — Magnetorquer. Coil that generates a magnetic dipole, used with Earth's magnetic field for attitude control.

searchDocs.title: N​
searchDocs.content: NADIR — Direction pointing toward Earth's center from the spacecraft.  NAND / NOR flash — Non-volatile storage technology. NAND is denser (used for data storage), NOR is byte-addressable (used for code execution).

searchDocs.title: O​
searchDocs.content: OBC — On-Board Computer. The main flight computer.  OSAL — OS Abstraction Layer. NASA cFS component that provides RTOS-independent API.  OBSW — On-Board Software. European term for flight software.

searchDocs.title: P​
searchDocs.content: PID — Proportional-Integral-Derivative. Common control law.  PSP — Platform Support Package. NASA cFS component providing board-specific abstraction.  PTT — Pass-Through Telemetry. Raw data relayed without processing.

searchDocs.title: Q​
searchDocs.content: Quaternion — 4-element rotation representation. Preferred for spacecraft attitude because it has no singularities.

searchDocs.title: R​
searchDocs.content: RAAN — Right Ascension of Ascending Node. Orbital element defining the orientation of the orbital plane relative to the vernal equinox.  RAD750 — Radiation-hardened PowerPC processor from BAE Systems. The most widely used space processor for high-reliability missions.  RW — Reaction Wheel. Electric motor with a flywheel. Stores angular momentum to control spacecraft attitude.  RTOS — Real-Time Operating System.

searchDocs.title: S​
searchDocs.content: SAA — South Atlantic Anomaly. Region over the South Atlantic where the inner Van Allen belt dips closest to Earth's surface. Highest SEU rates for LEO spacecraft.  SBand — Frequency band (2–4 GHz). Commonly used for spacecraft telemetry and command.  SB — Software Bus (in cFS context).  SEE — Single Event Effect. Radiation-induced effect in electronics.  SEL — Single Event Latchup. Destructive radiation effect.  SEU — Single Event Upset. Non-destructive bit flip from radiation.  SGP4 — Simplified General Perturbations model 4. Standard algorithm for propagating satellite TLEs.  SRAM — Static RAM. Volatile, fast memory used for program data. More radiation-tolerant than DRAM.  SSO — Sun-Synchronous Orbit. Polar orbit precessing at the same rate the Earth orbits the Sun — the spacecraft always crosses the equator at the same local solar time.

searchDocs.title: T​
searchDocs.content: TAI — International Atomic Time. Continuous time scale, no leap seconds.  TBL — Table (in cFS context). Configurable data structure updatable via uplink.  TID — Total Ionizing Dose. Cumulative radiation damage over mission life.  TLE — Two-Line Element set. Standardized format for encoding Keplerian orbital elements.  TMR — Triple Modular Redundancy.  TT&C — Telemetry, Tracking, and Command.

searchDocs.title: U​
searchDocs.content: UHF — Ultra High Frequency (300 MHz – 3 GHz). Used for backup command uplink, CubeSat communications.  UTC — Coordinated Universal Time. Civil time standard, includes leap seconds.

searchDocs.title: V​
searchDocs.content: VSCMG — Variable Speed Control Moment Gyroscope.  VxWorks — Commercial RTOS by Wind River, widely used in avionics and spacecraft.

searchDocs.title: W​
searchDocs.content: WCET — Worst-Case Execution Time. Maximum time a function or task can take to execute, used in real-time scheduling analysis.  WMM — World Magnetic Model. NOAA/BGS model of Earth's magnetic field. Used by ADCS software to determine expected magnetometer reading given position and time.

searchDocs.title: X​
searchDocs.content: Xband — Frequency band (8–12 GHz). Used for high-rate data downlink from imaging satellites.

searchDocs.title: Launch Vehicles​
searchDocs.content: Companies that build rockets. Their FSW problems: flight control, engine management, vehicle health monitoring, ground software.

searchDocs.title: Firefly Aerospace (Austin, TX)​
searchDocs.content: Alpha and Beta launch vehicles targeting small-to-medium payloads. Growing from a near-failure to successful orbital launches. FSW stack includes flight computer software, telemetry systems, and ground software.  Software focus: Flight control software, avionics FSW, ground systems, mission operations.

searchDocs.title: Relativity Space (Long Beach, CA)​
searchDocs.content: 3D-printed launch vehicles (Terran 1, Terran R). Novel manufacturing but conventional avionics challenges — plus the software to control metal 3D printers at aerospace scale.  Software focus: Flight software, GNC (guidance, navigation, control), manufacturing software (Python/C++ for print process control).

searchDocs.title: Rocket Lab (Long Beach, CA / Auckland, NZ)​
searchDocs.content: Electron (small launch, proven) and Neutron (medium, in development). First-stage reuse via helicopter catch. Very active launch cadence.  Software focus: Flight software (C/C++), GNC, avionics, ground systems, mission operations. Well-known for solid engineering culture.

searchDocs.title: SpaceX (Hawthorne, CA)​
searchDocs.content: Falcon 9, Falcon Heavy, Starship. Also Starlink constellation (4,000+ satellites). Most prolific launch provider in the world.  Software focus: FSW, GNC, Starlink operations software, ground systems. Uses C++ heavily. Competitive, fast-paced.

searchDocs.title: Spire Global (San Francisco, CA)​
searchDocs.content: Constellation of 100+ small satellites (3U/6U CubeSats) providing weather, maritime, and aviation data. One of the largest CubeSat operators.  Software focus: Flight software for small sats, ground software, data pipeline. Good entry point for FSW with CubeSat-scale complexity.

searchDocs.title: Planet Labs (San Francisco, CA)​
searchDocs.content: World's largest constellation of Earth observation satellites (Doves, SuperDoves). Daily imaging of the entire Earth.  Software focus: FSW, operations automation, data processing pipelines, image processing. Mix of embedded and backend engineering.

searchDocs.title: Axelspace (Tokyo, Japan)​
searchDocs.content: Small satellite manufacturer and Earth observation operator (GRUS constellation). Growing presence in commercial small sat market.  Software focus: FSW development, satellite operations. Japanese aerospace market entry point.

searchDocs.title: Satellogic (Buenos Aires / New York)​
searchDocs.content: High-resolution, low-cost Earth observation constellation. Focused on making satellite imagery affordable.  Software focus: FSW, mission operations, data products.

searchDocs.title: Maxar Technologies (Westminster, CO)​
searchDocs.content: Operator of WorldView imaging satellites. Also builds satellites (buses, instruments) for government and commercial customers.  Software focus: FSW for large, complex imaging satellites. More traditional aerospace culture.

searchDocs.title: OneWeb / Eutelsat (London / Paris)​
searchDocs.content: LEO broadband constellation (648 satellites). Starlink competitor.  Software focus: Constellation management software, ground systems, link management.

searchDocs.title: Iridium (McLean, VA)​
searchDocs.content: 66-satellite LEO constellation providing global voice and data. The original LEO constellation, relaunched in 2019.  Software focus: Network operations, satellite control software.

searchDocs.title: SKY Perfect JSAT (Tokyo, Japan)​
searchDocs.content: Japan's dominant satellite operator. GEO communications satellites, growing into LEO operations. Also operating the "Space Compass" LEO infrastructure initiative.  Software focus: Satellite operations, ground segment software, network management.

searchDocs.title: Northrop Grumman​
searchDocs.content: James Webb Space Telescope (bus), MEV satellite servicing vehicles, defense systems.

searchDocs.title: Lockheed Martin (Space division, Denver, CO)​
searchDocs.content: Orion crew capsule, GPS III, A2100 bus. Large program culture.

searchDocs.title: Raytheon Intelligence & Space​
searchDocs.content: Classified and commercial satellite programs.

searchDocs.title: L3Harris Technologies​
searchDocs.content: Communications satellites, missile warning systems.

searchDocs.title: Ansys (formerly AGI) — Systems Tool Kit (STK)​
searchDocs.content: The standard tool for mission analysis, coverage analysis, and link budget calculations. Not hardware, but used universally.

searchDocs.title: Mathworks — Matlab/Simulink​
searchDocs.content: GNC algorithm development, auto-code generation (Embedded Coder). ADCS algorithms often prototyped here before C++ implementation.

searchDocs.title: Wind River — VxWorks​
searchDocs.content: The dominant commercial RTOS for avionics and space. VxWorks 653 is DO-178C certifiable.

searchDocs.title: What They Actually Hire For​
searchDocs.content: Role	What You NeedFlight Software Engineer	C/C++, RTOS (FreeRTOS/RTEMS/VxWorks), cFS, embedded debugging GNC Engineer	Orbital mechanics, Kalman filtering, MATLAB/Simulink, C++ implementation Ground Software Engineer	Python, C++, distributed systems, real-time data pipelines Systems Engineer	Systems architecture, requirements, interface definition, verification FPGA Engineer	VHDL/Verilog or HLS, radiation-hardened FPGAs, high-speed interfaces  The most direct path from this reference to employment is Flight Software Engineer — the role that directly implements what this reference teaches.

searchDocs.title: Architecture Overview​
searchDocs.content: cFS is built on three core components:  Component	PurposeOSAL (OS Abstraction Layer)	Abstracts RTOS — one API for FreeRTOS, RTEMS, POSIX Linux PSP (Platform Support Package)	Board-specific abstraction — memory map, startup, timers cFE (Core Flight Executive)	Runtime services: Software Bus, Time, Events, Tables, Executive  Applications (apps) run on top of cFE and communicate exclusively through the Software Bus.  ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ │ ADCS │ │ EPS │ │ Comms │ │ HK App │ │ App │ │ App │ │ App │ │ │ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬─────┘ │ │ │ │ └───────────┴───────────┴───────────┘ │ ┌───────▼───────┐ │ Software Bus │ ← publish/subscribe message bus └───────┬───────┘ │ ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌────▼───┐ ┌─────▼──┐ ┌─────▼──┐ │ EVS │ │ TIME │ │ TBL │ │ Events │ │ Service│ │ Tables │ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ ┌─────────────┐ │ OSAL │ └──────┬──────┘ ┌──────▼──────┐ │ PSP │ └──────┬──────┘ ┌──────▼──────┐ │ RTOS │ └─────────────┘

searchDocs.title: The Software Bus (SB)​
searchDocs.content: The Software Bus is cFS's central message routing system. Applications communicate by publishing messages to the bus and subscribing to message IDs they care about. No app has a direct dependency on any other app.  Message IDs (MID) are 16-bit values defined in a mission header:  // mission_msgids.h #define ADCS_CMD_MID 0x1880 // commands TO the ADCS app #define ADCS_TELEMETRY_MID 0x0880 // telemetry FROM the ADCS app #define HK_SEND_HK_MID 0x18B0 // housekeeping request   Sending a message:  // In ADCS app — publish attitude telemetry ADCS_HkTlm_t hk_packet; // Fill telemetry hk_packet.q[0] = attitude.q0; hk_packet.q[1] = attitude.q1; hk_packet.q[2] = attitude.q2; hk_packet.q[3] = attitude.q3; hk_packet.mode = current_mode; // Set CCSDS header CFE_SB_TimeStampMsg(CFE_MSG_PTR(hk_packet.TelemetryHeader)); CFE_SB_TransmitMsg(CFE_MSG_PTR(hk_packet.TelemetryHeader), true);   Receiving messages:  // In ADCS app main loop — wait for messages void ADCS_AppMain(void) { CFE_SB_Buffer_t* SBBufPtr; // Subscribe to messages this app cares about CFE_SB_Subscribe(ADCS_CMD_MID, ADCS_Global.CmdPipe); CFE_SB_Subscribe(HK_SEND_HK_MID, ADCS_Global.CmdPipe); while (1) { // Block waiting for a message on our pipe CFE_SB_ReceiveBuffer(&SBBufPtr, ADCS_Global.CmdPipe, CFE_SB_PEND_FOREVER); CFE_MSG_FcnCode_t FcnCode; CFE_MSG_GetFcnCode(&SBBufPtr->Msg, &FcnCode); switch (CFE_MSG_GetMsgId(&SBBufPtr->Msg)) { case ADCS_CMD_MID: ADCS_ProcessCmd(SBBufPtr); break; case HK_SEND_HK_MID: ADCS_SendHkTelemetry(); break; default: CFE_EVS_SendEvent(ADCS_MID_ERR_EID, CFE_EVS_EventType_ERROR, "Unknown MID: 0x%04X", CFE_MSG_GetMsgId(&SBBufPtr->Msg)); break; } } }

searchDocs.title: Event Service (EVS)​
searchDocs.content: EVS provides a standardized logging and event system. Apps report events (informational, debug, errors, critical) through EVS rather than printf.  // Register events at app init CFE_EVS_Register(ADCS_EventFilters, ADCS_EVENT_COUNT, CFE_EVS_EventFilter_BINARY); // Log events CFE_EVS_SendEvent(ADCS_INIT_INF_EID, CFE_EVS_EventType_INFORMATION, "ADCS App initialized, version %d.%d", ADCS_MAJOR_VERSION, ADCS_MINOR_VERSION); CFE_EVS_SendEvent(ADCS_CMD_ERR_EID, CFE_EVS_EventType_ERROR, "Invalid command code: %d", cmd_code);   EVS routes events to the ground via telemetry and to on-board storage — it's the spacecraft equivalent of a logging framework.

searchDocs.title: Time Service (TIME)​
searchDocs.content: All apps use cFE TIME for timestamps — never gettimeofday() or a bare hardware timer. TIME maintains spacecraft mission elapsed time (MET) and can be synchronized with GPS or ground uplink.  CFE_TIME_SysTime_t timestamp = CFE_TIME_GetTime(); // timestamp.Seconds: seconds since mission epoch // timestamp.Subseconds: fractional seconds (2^-32 second units)

searchDocs.title: Table Service (TBL)​
searchDocs.content: Parameters that may need to be updated in flight (PID gains, telemetry limits, attitude target) are stored in TBL tables. Tables can be uplinked and applied without restarting the app.  // Load table at init CFE_TBL_Register(&ADCS_Global.GainsTableHandle, "ADCS.Gains", sizeof(ADCS_GainsTbl_t), CFE_TBL_OPT_DEFAULT, ADCS_GainsTableValidate); CFE_TBL_Load(ADCS_Global.GainsTableHandle, CFE_TBL_SRC_FILE, "/cf/adcs_gains.tbl"); // Use table data ADCS_GainsTbl_t* gains_ptr; CFE_TBL_GetAddress((void**)&gains_ptr, ADCS_Global.GainsTableHandle); double kp = gains_ptr->kp; CFE_TBL_ReleaseAddress(ADCS_Global.GainsTableHandle);   This is how you change PID gains on a spacecraft in orbit without a software update.

searchDocs.title: OSAL — Writing Portable Flight Software​
searchDocs.content: OSAL (OS Abstraction Layer) provides a single API that works on FreeRTOS, RTEMS, VxWorks, and POSIX Linux. Flight software written against OSAL can run on any of them.  // Create a task — same code regardless of underlying RTOS OS_TaskCreate( &task_id, // output: task ID "ADCS_CTRL", // name ADCS_ControlTask, // function pointer stack_ptr, // stack buffer stack_size, // stack size ADCS_CTRL_PRIORITY, // priority 0 // flags ); // Semaphores OS_BinSemCreate(&sem_id, "DATA_RDY", 0, 0); OS_BinSemGive(sem_id); // from ISR or producer OS_BinSemTake(sem_id); // blocks consumer   The same application code runs on the embedded target and on a Linux workstation for testing. This is the primary value proposition of cFS for ground test and continuous integration.

searchDocs.title: App Lifecycle​
searchDocs.content: Every cFS app implements the same three-phase lifecycle:  void MY_AppMain(void) { uint32 RunStatus = CFE_ES_RunStatus_APP_RUN; // 1. Initialize MY_AppInit(); // 2. Run — main loop while (CFE_ES_RunLoop(&RunStatus) == true) { CFE_SB_Buffer_t* BufPtr; CFE_SB_ReceiveBuffer(&BufPtr, MY_Global.CmdPipe, MY_TIMEOUT_MSEC); MY_ProcessMsg(BufPtr); } // 3. Exit — clean up MY_AppCleanup(); CFE_ES_ExitApp(RunStatus); }   The CFE_ES_RunLoop() check allows the Executive Service to command an app to stop gracefully — critical for in-flight software restarts.

searchDocs.title: Why cFS Matters​
searchDocs.content: Before cFS, every mission wrote FSW from scratch. The same command handler, telemetry packer, and housekeeping app were rewritten for each mission — and each rewrite introduced new bugs.  cFS solves this by:  Providing battle-tested, reusable infrastructure apps (command router, telemetry output, housekeeping, stored commands)Defining a standard interface (SB messages + CCSDS headers) that makes apps composableEnabling desktop simulation — test your app on Linux before the hardware existsAccelerating new mission development — focus engineering time on mission-unique software, not plumbing

searchDocs.title: The Three Levels​
searchDocs.content: FDIR operates at three levels, each faster and more drastic than the last:

searchDocs.title: Level 1 — Component Level (milliseconds)​
searchDocs.content: Individual subsystems monitor themselves and attempt self-recovery.  // Example: UART driver with auto-retry static bool uart_send_with_retry(const uint8_t* data, size_t len) { for (int attempt = 0; attempt < 3; attempt++) { if (uart_transmit(data, len, UART_TIMEOUT_MS) == UART_OK) { return true; } log_event(EVENT_UART_RETRY, attempt, 0); uart_reset(); // reset peripheral and try again } // Level 1 exhausted — escalate to Level 2 fdir_report_anomaly(ANOMALY_UART_PERSISTENT_FAILURE, UART_COMMS); return false; }

searchDocs.title: Level 2 — Subsystem Level (seconds)​
searchDocs.content: The FDIR system receives anomaly reports and applies subsystem-level responses.

searchDocs.title: Level 3 — Spacecraft Level (minutes)​
searchDocs.content: If anomalies escalate, the spacecraft transitions to safe mode — a minimal, low-power configuration designed to survive indefinitely while waiting for ground contact.

searchDocs.title: Anomaly Reporting​
searchDocs.content: All software components report faults to a central FDIR service through a standardized interface:  // Anomaly IDs — defines what went wrong typedef enum { ANOMALY_NONE = 0, ANOMALY_IMU_FAILURE = 1, ANOMALY_MAGNETOMETER_FAILURE = 2, ANOMALY_RW_SPEED_LIMIT = 3, ANOMALY_ECC_UNCORRECTABLE = 4, ANOMALY_TMR_FAILURE = 5, ANOMALY_COMMAND_AUTH_FAIL = 6, ANOMALY_POWER_UNDERVOLTAGE = 7, ANOMALY_COMMS_LINK_LOST = 8, ANOMALY_WATCHDOG_IMPENDING = 9, ANOMALY_HIGH_CE_RATE = 10, ANOMALY_LATCHUP_DETECTED = 11, ANOMALY_PERSISTENT_LATCHUP = 12, // ... ANOMALY_COUNT } AnomalyID_t; // Severity determines FDIR response speed typedef enum { SEVERITY_INFO = 0, // log only — no action SEVERITY_WARNING = 1, // log + telemetry alert SEVERITY_FAULT = 2, // active recovery attempt SEVERITY_CRITICAL = 3, // immediate safe mode } AnomalySeverity_t; typedef struct { AnomalyID_t id; AnomalySeverity_t severity; uint32_t param1; uint32_t param2; uint64_t timestamp_us; } AnomalyReport_t; // Thread-safe reporting — can be called from any task or ISR void fdir_report_anomaly(AnomalyID_t id, uint32_t param1) { AnomalyReport_t report; report.id = id; report.severity = ANOMALY_SEVERITY_TABLE[id]; report.param1 = param1; report.param2 = 0; report.timestamp_us = get_mission_time_us(); // Send to FDIR queue — non-blocking from ISR context BaseType_t xWoken = pdFALSE; if (xPortIsInsideInterrupt()) { xQueueSendFromISR(fdir_queue, &report, &xWoken); portYIELD_FROM_ISR(xWoken); } else { xQueueSend(fdir_queue, &report, 0); // don't block — just drop if full } }

searchDocs.title: The FDIR Task​
searchDocs.content: // FDIR task — high priority, processes anomaly reports void vFDIR_Task(void* pvParams) { AnomalyReport_t report; for (;;) { // Block waiting for anomaly reports if (xQueueReceive(fdir_queue, &report, portMAX_DELAY) == pdTRUE) { fdir_process_anomaly(&report); } } } // Response table — maps anomaly IDs to recovery actions typedef void (*RecoveryAction_t)(const AnomalyReport_t* report); static const RecoveryAction_t RECOVERY_TABLE[ANOMALY_COUNT] = { [ANOMALY_IMU_FAILURE] = fdir_handle_imu_failure, [ANOMALY_MAGNETOMETER_FAILURE] = fdir_handle_mag_failure, [ANOMALY_RW_SPEED_LIMIT] = fdir_handle_rw_saturation, [ANOMALY_ECC_UNCORRECTABLE] = fdir_handle_ecc_failure, [ANOMALY_POWER_UNDERVOLTAGE] = fdir_handle_undervoltage, [ANOMALY_COMMS_LINK_LOST] = fdir_handle_link_lost, // ... }; static void fdir_process_anomaly(const AnomalyReport_t* report) { // Log the anomaly — always event_log_write(report); // Increment anomaly counter anomaly_counts[report->id]++; // Check for repeated failure (component degraded) if (anomaly_counts[report->id] > ANOMALY_THRESHOLD[report->id]) { // Escalate to safe mode fdir_trigger_safe_mode(SAFE_MODE_REASON_REPEATED_FAULT, report->id); return; } // Execute recovery action if (RECOVERY_TABLE[report->id] != NULL) { RECOVERY_TABLE[report->id](report); } // Critical severity → immediate safe mode if (report->severity == SEVERITY_CRITICAL) { fdir_trigger_safe_mode(SAFE_MODE_REASON_CRITICAL_FAULT, report->id); } }

searchDocs.title: Safe Mode​
searchDocs.content: Safe mode is the spacecraft's "lifeboat" configuration — the minimum set of subsystems needed to survive and communicate with the ground.  // Safe mode entry — called from FDIR or autonomy rules void fdir_trigger_safe_mode(SafeModeReason_t reason, uint32_t detail) { // Log the trigger log_event(EVENT_SAFE_MODE_ENTRY, (uint32_t)reason, detail); persist_safe_mode_cause(reason, detail); // survive reset // 1. Command spacecraft to detumble / sun-pointing attitude // (reduces power consumption, ensures solar charging) adcs_set_mode(ADCS_MODE_BDOT_DETUMBLE); // 2. Disable non-essential loads eps_set_load_shedding(LOAD_SHED_SAFE_MODE); // → off: science instruments, non-critical sensors, heaters // → on: OBC, comms, GPS, IMU, reaction wheels // 3. Reduce transmission rate — conserve power comms_set_data_rate(COMMS_RATE_BEACON_ONLY); // 4. Set mode register set_spacecraft_mode(MODE_SAFE); // 5. Suspend non-critical tasks vTaskSuspend(science_task_handle); vTaskSuspend(data_storage_task_handle); // DO NOT suspend: ADCS, FDIR, watchdog kick, comms RX }

searchDocs.title: Watchdog Integration​
searchDocs.content: The most important fault response mechanism is the hardware watchdog — a timer that resets the processor if not kicked within a set interval. If software hangs (deadlock, infinite loop, hard fault), the watchdog fires and recovers the system.  // Watchdog kick task — highest priority, runs every 500 ms // If ANY task deadlocks and holds a resource that blocks this task, // the watchdog fires, resetting the system void vWatchdogKickTask(void* pvParams) { for (;;) { // Only kick if all critical tasks are healthy if (is_adcs_task_healthy() && is_fdir_task_healthy()) { hardware_watchdog_kick(); } // If we can't kick, watchdog fires and resets system → safe vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(500)); } }   See Watchdog Patterns for multi-level watchdog architectures.

searchDocs.title: FDIR Design Principles​
searchDocs.content: 1. Conservative by default. When in doubt, go to safe mode. A ground operator can recover a spacecraft in safe mode. They may not be able to recover a spacecraft that suffered damage because FDIR kept running a failing system.  2. No infinite loops in recovery. Every recovery action must have a maximum retry count and an escalation path.  3. Preserve diagnostic information. Before resetting or transitioning to safe mode, write the cause to non-volatile memory. This is often the only way to diagnose the problem later.  4. Ground must be able to override. FDIR should be configurable — autonomous responses should be enable/disable-able by ground command, with adjustable thresholds.  5. Test your fault responses. Fault injection testing — deliberately triggering anomaly conditions in test — is the only way to verify FDIR works correctly.

searchDocs.title: Hard vs. Soft Real-Time​
searchDocs.content: Type	Definition	ExampleHard real-time	Missing a deadline is a system failure	Attitude control loop, thruster firing, safety cutoffs Soft real-time	Missing a deadline degrades quality, not correctness	Telemetry compression, non-critical logging Firm real-time	Late results are useless but not catastrophic	Video frame processing, display updates  Most spacecraft flight software is hard real-time. The attitude determination and control system (ADCS) must run at a fixed rate — if it misses a cycle, the spacecraft may tumble. A fault management system must detect anomalies within a bounded time — a late detection could allow hardware damage.

searchDocs.title: What an RTOS Provides​
searchDocs.content: At minimum, an RTOS provides:  Preemptive task scheduler — tasks are assigned priorities; higher-priority tasks preempt lower-priority tasks immediatelyDeterministic context switch — the time to switch between tasks is bounded and knownIPC primitives — queues, semaphores, mutexes for safe inter-task communicationTimer services — periodic task activation, delays, timeoutsInterrupt management — integration between hardware interrupts and software tasks

searchDocs.title: The Preemptive Scheduler​
searchDocs.content: Unlike a superloop (see Bare Metal C), an RTOS scheduler can interrupt a running task mid-execution and switch to a higher-priority task. This is the key mechanism that makes hard real-time behavior possible.  Priority 3 (high): ▓▓░░░░░▓▓░░░░░▓▓ Attitude control — runs every 10 ms Priority 2 (med): ░░░▓▓░░░░░▓▓░░░░ Telemetry — runs when not preempted Priority 1 (low): ░░░░░▓░░░░░░▓░░░ Background processing ────────────────── time →   The highest-priority ready task always runs. Lower-priority tasks run only when no higher-priority task is ready.

searchDocs.content: RTOS	NotesFreeRTOS	Open source, widely used in CubeSats and commercial small sat RTEMS	NASA's choice — used on Mars rovers, open source, POSIX-compatible VxWorks	Commercial, long heritage in avionics and deep space (Voyager, Mars rovers via VME) Zephyr	Modern open source, growing aerospace adoption ThreadX	Commercial, small footprint, deterministic INTEGRITY	High-assurance RTOS, used in safety-critical aerospace

searchDocs.title: The Problem​
searchDocs.content: You have multiple tasks with timing requirements:  Task	Period	Deadline	Execution TimeAttitude control	10 ms	10 ms	2 ms Fault monitor	50 ms	50 ms	1 ms Telemetry packetizer	100 ms	100 ms	5 ms Command handler	200 ms	200 ms	3 ms  Can these all be scheduled to meet their deadlines? And which scheduling algorithm should you use?

searchDocs.title: Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)​
searchDocs.content: The rule: Assign priorities based on period. Shorter period = higher priority.  This is the most common algorithm in practice and maps directly to FreeRTOS/RTEMS priority assignment. It is optimal for fixed-priority scheduling of periodic tasks with deadlines equal to their periods.  For the tasks above:  Attitude control (10 ms) → priority 4 (highest)Fault monitor (50 ms) → priority 3Telemetry (100 ms) → priority 2Command handler (200 ms) → priority 1  Schedulability test: A task set is schedulable under RMS if:  U=∑i=1nCiTi≤n(21/n−1)U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)U=∑i=1n​Ti​Ci​​≤n(21/n−1)  Where CiC_iCi​ is execution time, TiT_iTi​ is period, and nnn is the number of tasks.  For large nnn, the bound approaches ln⁡2≈0.693\ln 2 \approx 0.693ln2≈0.693. If total CPU utilization is below 69%, the task set is guaranteed schedulable under RMS.  For our example:U=210+150+5100+3200=0.2+0.02+0.05+0.015=0.285U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{1}{50} + \frac{5}{100} + \frac{3}{200} = 0.2 + 0.02 + 0.05 + 0.015 = 0.285U=102​+501​+1005​+2003​=0.2+0.02+0.05+0.015=0.285  Well below 0.693 — all tasks are guaranteed to meet their deadlines.

searchDocs.title: Priority Inversion​
searchDocs.content: Priority inversion occurs when a high-priority task is blocked by a low-priority task holding a resource the high-priority task needs.  Classic scenario:  Time → T_high (P3): [blocked waiting for mutex]─────────────────▓▓▓ T_med (P2): ░░░░░░░░▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ T_low (P1): ░░▓▓[holds mutex]────────────[releases mutex]░░   T_low holds a mutex. T_high needs it. T_high blocks. T_med (which doesn't need the mutex) preempts T_low and runs, indirectly delaying T_high — a high-priority task is blocked by a medium-priority task through a low-priority intermediary.  This was responsible for the Mars Pathfinder priority inversion (1997) — the spacecraft's watchdog reset because a high-priority telemetry task was starved by medium-priority tasks while waiting for a mutex held by a low-priority task.

searchDocs.title: Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)​
searchDocs.content: When T_high blocks on a mutex held by T_low, T_low temporarily inherits T_high's priority:  T_high (P3): [blocked]────────────────────────▓▓▓ T_med (P2): ░░░░[preempted by T_low*]░░░░░░░░░░░ T_low* (P1→P3): ░░▓▓[inherits P3, completes quickly]░   T_low runs at P3, finishes, releases the mutex. T_high unblocks immediately. T_med never gets to run during the critical section.  FreeRTOS mutexes implement priority inheritance. Semaphores do not — this is a key reason to use mutexes, not semaphores, for mutual exclusion.

searchDocs.title: Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)​
searchDocs.content: A stronger alternative: each mutex is assigned a priority ceiling equal to the highest priority of any task that ever acquires it. A task can only acquire a mutex if its priority is higher than the ceiling of all currently held mutexes.  PCP prevents all deadlocks and bounds the blocking time to at most one critical section. RTEMS supports PCP. FreeRTOS does not.

searchDocs.title: Earliest Deadline First (EDF)​
searchDocs.content: Dynamic scheduling algorithm: at each scheduling point, the task with the nearest absolute deadline runs.  EDF is theoretically optimal — it can schedule any task set with U≤1.0U \leq 1.0U≤1.0 (100% CPU utilization). RMS can only guarantee schedulability up to ~69%.  Why isn't EDF used everywhere?  Requires dynamic priority management — more overheadBehavior under overload is unpredictable — when U>1U > 1U>1, tasks miss deadlines in hard-to-predict waysHarder to analyze and certifyPriority inversion analysis is more complex  Most spacecraft flight software uses RMS with fixed priorities. EDF sees more use in real-time Linux systems.

searchDocs.title: The Mars Pathfinder Incident​
searchDocs.content: In 1997, the Mars Pathfinder spacecraft began experiencing unexpected resets shortly after landing. The spacecraft's watchdog was resetting the system because the high-priority meteorological data bus task was missing its deadline.  Root cause: classic unbounded priority inversion.  A low-priority task held the VxWorks IPC mutexA medium-priority task preempted it and ran for an extended periodThe high-priority bus task blocked waiting for the mutexThe watchdog fired because the bus task missed its deadline  Fix: enable priority inheritance on the mutex (VxWorks supported it but it was not configured by default). The fix was uplinked to the spacecraft and resolved the issue.  Lesson: Priority inheritance must be explicitly configured, not assumed. Test your synchronization patterns under realistic load before launch.

searchDocs.title: Practical Priority Assignment for Spacecraft​
searchDocs.content: // Priority assignments for a typical small satellite FSW // Higher number = higher priority (FreeRTOS convention) // Safety-critical — must never be blocked #define TASK_PRIORITY_FAULT_DETECTION 7 // FDIR monitor #define TASK_PRIORITY_WATCHDOG_KICK 6 // ensures WDT is fed // Mission-critical — timing directly affects mission success #define TASK_PRIORITY_ATTITUDE_CONTROL 5 // 10 ms control loop #define TASK_PRIORITY_POWER_MANAGEMENT 4 // power state machine // Operational — important but tolerates some delay #define TASK_PRIORITY_COMMAND_HANDLER 3 // uplink command processing #define TASK_PRIORITY_COMMS_PROTOCOL 3 // link layer // Support — runs when higher priorities are idle #define TASK_PRIORITY_TELEMETRY 2 // housekeeping downlink #define TASK_PRIORITY_DATA_STORAGE 1 // file system writes #define TASK_PRIORITY_BACKGROUND 0 // diagnostics, not in FreeRTOS   Never assign the same priority to tasks that share resources — priority inversion analysis becomes significantly more complex.

searchDocs.title: Radiation​
searchDocs.content: Space is filled with high-energy charged particles: protons and electrons trapped in the Van Allen belts, cosmic rays from outside the solar system, and energetic particles from solar events.  These particles interact with microelectronics in two ways:  Single Event Effects (SEE):  SEU (Single Event Upset): A particle flips a bit in memory or a register. The most common effect. Non-destructive.SEL (Single Event Latchup): A particle triggers a parasitic transistor structure, causing a high-current condition. Can destroy the device if power is not cycled.SEFi (Single Event Functional Interrupt): Corrupts configuration registers or state machines, causing unexpected behavior.  Total Ionizing Dose (TID):  Cumulative radiation damage over the mission lifetimeDegrades transistor characteristics — threshold voltage shift, increased leakage currentRated in krad (Si) — a typical LEO satellite might accumulate 5–30 krad over 3 years

searchDocs.title: Thermal​
searchDocs.content: Spacecraft experience extreme thermal cycling as they move between eclipse (shadow) and sunlight. In LEO, an orbital period is ~94 minutes with ~35 minutes in eclipse.  Temperature ranges depend on orbit and thermal design, but −40°C to +85°C is common for electronics. Some components (star trackers, battery packs) have tighter ranges.  The software implication: Crystal oscillator frequency drifts with temperature. Clock stability assumptions baked into timing code may need thermal correction.

searchDocs.title: Vacuum​
searchDocs.content: No atmosphere means no convective cooling. Heat can only leave the spacecraft by radiation. This affects the thermal design (heat pipes, radiators, coatings) but also the electronics — components designed for conductive cooling on Earth may overheat in vacuum.

searchDocs.title: Core Abstractions​
searchDocs.content: FreeRTOS gives you five building blocks:  Abstraction	PurposeTask	Independent thread of execution with its own stack Queue	FIFO message passing between tasks and ISRs Semaphore	Signaling and mutual exclusion Mutex	Mutual exclusion with priority inheritance Timer	Software timers for periodic or one-shot callbacks

searchDocs.title: Tasks​
searchDocs.content: A FreeRTOS task is a C function that never returns (it runs in an infinite loop or explicitly deletes itself).  // Task function signature void vMyTask(void* pvParameters) { // Initialization const TickType_t xDelay = pdMS_TO_TICKS(10); // 10 ms period for (;;) { // Do work read_sensors(); run_attitude_estimator(); // Block until next activation — this is NOT a busy-wait vTaskDelayUntil(&xLastWakeTime, xDelay); } // Never reached — but if task exits, it must delete itself vTaskDelete(NULL); } // Create the task TaskHandle_t xTaskHandle = NULL; xTaskCreate( vMyTask, // task function "ADCS", // name (for debugging) 512, // stack size in words (not bytes) NULL, // parameter passed to task 3, // priority (higher = more urgent) &xTaskHandle // handle for later reference );   Priority: Higher number = higher priority in FreeRTOS. Task with highest priority among ready tasks runs first. On a spacecraft, assign priorities according to timing criticality:  #define PRIORITY_ATTITUDE_CONTROL 5 // highest — 10 ms deadline #define PRIORITY_FAULT_MANAGER 4 // safety monitoring #define PRIORITY_COMMAND_HANDLER 3 // ground commands #define PRIORITY_TELEMETRY 2 // housekeeping downlink #define PRIORITY_BACKGROUND 1 // non-critical logging

searchDocs.title: Queues​
searchDocs.content: Queues are the primary mechanism for passing data between tasks and from ISRs to tasks. They are thread-safe by design — no external locking required.  // Create a queue that holds 10 sensor_reading_t items QueueHandle_t xSensorQueue = xQueueCreate(10, sizeof(sensor_reading_t)); // Producer: ISR or sensor task void vSensorTask(void* pvParams) { sensor_reading_t reading; for (;;) { reading = read_imu(); // Send to queue — block up to 10 ms if full xQueueSend(xSensorQueue, &reading, pdMS_TO_TICKS(10)); vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(10)); // 100 Hz sample rate } } // Consumer: attitude estimator task void vEstimatorTask(void* pvParams) { sensor_reading_t reading; for (;;) { // Block indefinitely waiting for data if (xQueueReceive(xSensorQueue, &reading, portMAX_DELAY) == pdTRUE) { run_ekf_update(&reading); } } }   From an ISR: Use xQueueSendFromISR() — the regular xQueueSend() must not be called from interrupt context.  void UART_IRQHandler(void) { uint8_t byte = UART_ReadByte(); BaseType_t xHigherPriorityTaskWoken = pdFALSE; xQueueSendFromISR(xRxQueue, &byte, &xHigherPriorityTaskWoken); // If the send unblocked a higher-priority task, yield to it portYIELD_FROM_ISR(xHigherPriorityTaskWoken); }

searchDocs.title: Semaphores​
searchDocs.content: Binary semaphore — signal from ISR to task:  SemaphoreHandle_t xDataReadySem = xSemaphoreCreateBinary(); // ISR: new data available void SENSOR_IRQHandler(void) { BaseType_t xWoken = pdFALSE; xSemaphoreGiveFromISR(xDataReadySem, &xWoken); portYIELD_FROM_ISR(xWoken); } // Task: wait for signal then process void vProcessTask(void* pvParams) { for (;;) { xSemaphoreTake(xDataReadySem, portMAX_DELAY); process_sensor_data(); } }   Counting semaphore — tracking available resources or events.

searchDocs.title: Mutexes and Priority Inheritance​
searchDocs.content: Use a mutex (not a binary semaphore) for mutual exclusion between tasks. FreeRTOS mutexes implement priority inheritance — when a high-priority task blocks waiting for a mutex held by a low-priority task, the low-priority task temporarily inherits the higher priority to prevent priority inversion.  MutexHandle_t xI2CBus = xSemaphoreCreateMutex(); void vTask_IMU(void* pvParams) { for (;;) { // Acquire I2C bus if (xSemaphoreTake(xI2CBus, pdMS_TO_TICKS(5)) == pdTRUE) { imu_read_i2c(&imu_data); xSemaphoreGive(xI2CBus); } else { // Timeout — log anomaly log_anomaly(ANOMALY_I2C_TIMEOUT); } vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(10)); } }

searchDocs.title: vTaskDelayUntil — Periodic Task Activation​
searchDocs.content: vTaskDelay() delays for N ticks from the current time. vTaskDelayUntil() delays until an absolute tick count. For periodic tasks, always use vTaskDelayUntil() — it compensates for execution jitter and maintains accurate periodicity.  void vPeriodicTask(void* pvParams) { TickType_t xLastWakeTime = xTaskGetTickCount(); const TickType_t xPeriod = pdMS_TO_TICKS(10); // 100 Hz for (;;) { // Do work run_control_loop(); // Wait until 10 ms have elapsed since xLastWakeTime // xLastWakeTime is updated automatically vTaskDelayUntil(&xLastWakeTime, xPeriod); } }   If run_control_loop() takes 3 ms, the next activation is 7 ms later — always maintaining 10 ms between activations. With vTaskDelay(10), the period would be 10 ms + execution time = 13 ms.

searchDocs.title: FreeRTOS Configuration​
searchDocs.content: FreeRTOSConfig.h configures the kernel for your target. Key settings:  // FreeRTOSConfig.h #define configUSE_PREEMPTION 1 // preemptive scheduler #define configCPU_CLOCK_HZ 168000000UL #define configTICK_RATE_HZ 1000 // 1 ms tick #define configMAX_PRIORITIES 8 #define configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE 128 // words #define configTOTAL_HEAP_SIZE (32 * 1024) // 32 KB for FreeRTOS heap // Use static allocation — no dynamic memory for tasks/queues #define configSUPPORT_STATIC_ALLOCATION 1 #define configSUPPORT_DYNAMIC_ALLOCATION 0 // ban dynamic alloc in flight code // Stack overflow detection #define configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW 2 // fill pattern check void vApplicationStackOverflowHook(TaskHandle_t xTask, char* pcTaskName); // Runtime stats for debugging #define configGENERATE_RUN_TIME_STATS 1   configSUPPORT_DYNAMIC_ALLOCATION 0 is critical for flight code — it prevents tasks and queues from being created with dynamic memory allocation, forcing you to use static variants (xTaskCreateStatic, xQueueCreateStatic) where buffers are allocated at compile time.

searchDocs.title: Stack Overflow Detection​
searchDocs.content: FreeRTOS can detect stack overflows two ways:  Method 1: Check stack pointer on context switch — fast but misses overflows that recoverMethod 2: Fill stack with pattern at creation, check watermark — catches all overflows  // Called when a stack overflow is detected void vApplicationStackOverflowHook(TaskHandle_t xTask, char* pcTaskName) { // Log which task overflowed fault_log_write(FAULT_STACK_OVERFLOW, (uint32_t)xTask, 0); // Reset — the system is in an unknown state NVIC_SystemReset(); }   In development, use uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark() to check how close each task came to overflowing its stack, then size stacks with margin.

searchDocs.title: The Reliability Philosophy​
searchDocs.content: Safety-critical software engineering is built on three principles:  1. No undefined behavior. Every possible execution path must be analyzed. An unhandled edge case that crashes a web server sends an HTTP 500. An unhandled edge case in flight software may be unrecoverable.  2. Determinism. The software must behave identically given the same inputs, always. No reliance on timing coincidences, uninitialized memory, or platform-specific behavior.  3. Defense in depth. Assume hardware will fail. Assume software has bugs. Design the system so that a single failure — hardware or software — cannot cause a mission loss.

searchDocs.title: The Standards​
searchDocs.content: Standard	Domain	What It DefinesDO-178C	Avionics	Software development, verification, and certification levels ECSS-E-ST-40	Space (ESA)	Space software engineering process MISRA-C:2012	Embedded	C language subset for safety-critical code ISO 26262	Automotive	Functional safety for road vehicles IEC 61508	General	Functional safety of electrical/electronic systems  For spacecraft, DO-178C (originally for avionics) and ECSS-E-ST-40 (ESA) are the most relevant. NASA follows NPR 7150.2 for software requirements.

searchDocs.title: Development Assurance Levels​
searchDocs.content: DO-178C defines five Development Assurance Levels (DAL) based on the consequence of a software failure:  DAL	Consequence	Verification Rigor	ExampleA	Catastrophic — loss of life or vehicle	Highest	Flight control, propulsion cutoff B	Hazardous — severe injury or mission loss	High	Attitude control, power management C	Major — significant reduction in safety margins	Medium	Telemetry, command routing D	Minor — slight reduction in margins	Low	Non-critical logging, UI E	No effect	None	Ground software, development tools  Higher DAL requires more artifacts: more test coverage, structural coverage analysis (MC/DC at DAL A/B), independence between development and verification.

searchDocs.title: The Philosophy​
searchDocs.content: You can read about quaternion attitude representation, RTOS task scheduling, ECC memory, and CCSDS packets as separate topics. But in production flight software, they are not separate — they interact constantly.  The attitude control loop uses quaternions, reads from sensors over SPI bus, runs as a FreeRTOS task with a hard deadline, and its outputs must be ECC-protected because a single bit flip in a reaction wheel torque command could destabilize the spacecraft.  The synthesis section is where you see all of this together.
