searchDocs.title: Euler Angles — Intuitive but Dangerous​
searchDocs.content: Euler angles represent orientation as three successive rotations: roll, pitch, yaw. They are intuitive and easy to visualize.  The problem: gimbal lock.  At certain orientations, one rotational degree of freedom is lost. The most common form occurs when pitch reaches ±90° — roll and yaw become geometrically equivalent and the system loses the ability to represent orientation continuously.  typedef struct { double roll_rad; // φ double pitch_rad; // θ double yaw_rad; // ψ } EulerAngles_t; // The rotation matrix from body to reference (ZYX convention) void euler_to_dcm(const EulerAngles_t* e, double R[3][3]) { double cphi = cos(e->roll_rad); double sphi = sin(e->roll_rad); double ctheta = cos(e->pitch_rad); double stheta = sin(e->pitch_rad); double cpsi = cos(e->yaw_rad); double spsi = sin(e->yaw_rad); R[0][0] = ctheta*cpsi; R[0][1] = ctheta*spsi; R[0][2] = -stheta; R[1][0] = sphi*stheta*cpsi - cphi*spsi; R[1][1] = sphi*stheta*spsi + cphi*cpsi; R[1][2] = sphi*ctheta; R[2][0] = cphi*stheta*cpsi + sphi*spsi; R[2][1] = cphi*stheta*spsi - sphi*cpsi; R[2][2] = cphi*ctheta; }   When to use: Display, human-readable attitude reports, when you know you will never be near singularity. Never use Euler angles as your internal attitude representation in a control system.

searchDocs.title: Direction Cosine Matrix (DCM)​
searchDocs.content: A rotation matrix (3×3 orthogonal matrix, det = +1) directly encodes the rotation between two frames.  Rbodyref=(r11r12r13r21r22r23r31r32r33)R_{body}^{ref} = \begin{pmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{pmatrix}Rbodyref​=​r11​r21​r31​​r12​r22​r32​​r13​r23​r33​​​  To rotate a vector from body frame to reference frame: v⃗ref=R⋅v⃗body\vec{v}_{ref} = R \cdot \vec{v}_{body}vref​=R⋅vbody​  Advantages: No singularities. Direct geometric meaning. Easy to chain rotations: RAB=RAC⋅RCBR_{AB} = R_{AC} \cdot R_{CB}RAB​=RAC​⋅RCB​  Disadvantages: 9 elements for 3 degrees of freedom — over-parameterized. The orthogonality constraint (RTR=IR^T R = IRTR=I, det⁡(R)=1\det(R) = 1det(R)=1) drifts under numerical integration — must be periodically renormalized.  // Renormalize a DCM to maintain orthogonality // (orthogonalization via Gram-Schmidt) void dcm_renormalize(double R[3][3]) { // Row 1: normalize double n1 = sqrt(R[0][0]*R[0][0] + R[0][1]*R[0][1] + R[0][2]*R[0][2]); R[0][0] /= n1; R[0][1] /= n1; R[0][2] /= n1; // Row 2: subtract projection onto row 1, normalize double dot = R[0][0]*R[1][0] + R[0][1]*R[1][1] + R[0][2]*R[1][2]; R[1][0] -= dot*R[0][0]; R[1][1] -= dot*R[0][1]; R[1][2] -= dot*R[0][2]; double n2 = sqrt(R[1][0]*R[1][0] + R[1][1]*R[1][1] + R[1][2]*R[1][2]); R[1][0] /= n2; R[1][1] /= n2; R[1][2] /= n2; // Row 3: cross product of rows 1 and 2 (guaranteed orthogonal) R[2][0] = R[0][1]*R[1][2] - R[0][2]*R[1][1]; R[2][1] = R[0][2]*R[1][0] - R[0][0]*R[1][2]; R[2][2] = R[0][0]*R[1][1] - R[0][1]*R[1][0]; }

searchDocs.title: Quaternions — The Flight Software Standard​
searchDocs.content: A unit quaternion encodes a rotation as a 4-element vector with a single constraint: ∥q∥=1\|\mathbf{q}\| = 1∥q∥=1.  q=(q1q2q3q4)=(esin⁡(θ/2)cos⁡(θ/2))\mathbf{q} = \begin{pmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ q_3 \\ q_4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{e} \sin(\theta/2) \\ \cos(\theta/2) \end{pmatrix}q=​q1​q2​q3​q4​​​=(esin(θ/2)cos(θ/2)​)  Where e\mathbf{e}e is the unit axis of rotation and θ\thetaθ is the rotation angle.  Advantages:  4 elements for 3 DOF — more compact than DCMNo singularitiesFast multiplication (fewer operations than DCM composition)Easy to renormalize — just divide by magnitudeNumerically stable for integration  The double-cover problem: q\mathbf{q}q and −q-\mathbf{q}−q represent the same physical rotation. This can cause unwinding — the attitude controller taking the "long way around" through a 360° rotation. Must be handled explicitly.  typedef struct { double q1, q2, q3; // vector part: e * sin(theta/2) double q4; // scalar part: cos(theta/2) } Quaternion_t; // Quaternion multiplication: q_AB * q_BC = q_AC // (rotation A→B followed by B→C gives A→C) Quaternion_t quat_multiply(const Quaternion_t* p, const Quaternion_t* q) { Quaternion_t r; r.q1 = p->q4*q->q1 + p->q1*q->q4 + p->q2*q->q3 - p->q3*q->q2; r.q2 = p->q4*q->q2 - p->q1*q->q3 + p->q2*q->q4 + p->q3*q->q1; r.q3 = p->q4*q->q3 + p->q1*q->q2 - p->q2*q->q1 + p->q3*q->q4; r.q4 = p->q4*q->q4 - p->q1*q->q1 - p->q2*q->q2 - p->q3*q->q3; return r; } // Renormalize — do this periodically to prevent drift void quat_normalize(Quaternion_t* q) { double norm = sqrt(q->q1*q->q1 + q->q2*q->q2 + q->q3*q->q3 + q->q4*q->q4); q->q1 /= norm; q->q2 /= norm; q->q3 /= norm; q->q4 /= norm; } // Rotate a vector from body frame to reference frame void quat_rotate_vector(const Quaternion_t* q, const double v_body[3], double v_ref[3]) { // v_ref = q * [v|0] * q_conjugate (pure quaternion sandwich product) double q1=q->q1, q2=q->q2, q3=q->q3, q4=q->q4; double vx=v_body[0], vy=v_body[1], vz=v_body[2]; v_ref[0] = (1-2*(q2*q2+q3*q3))*vx + 2*(q1*q2-q3*q4)*vy + 2*(q1*q3+q2*q4)*vz; v_ref[1] = 2*(q1*q2+q3*q4)*vx + (1-2*(q1*q1+q3*q3))*vy + 2*(q2*q3-q1*q4)*vz; v_ref[2] = 2*(q1*q3-q2*q4)*vx + 2*(q2*q3+q1*q4)*vy + (1-2*(q1*q1+q2*q2))*vz; }

searchDocs.title: Quaternion Integration​
searchDocs.content: Given angular velocity ω\boldsymbol{\omega}ω in body frame, propagate the quaternion:  q˙=12q⊗(ω0)\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{q} \otimes \begin{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{\omega} \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}q˙​=21​q⊗(ω0​)  // Integrate quaternion given angular rate (rad/s) and time step (s) void quat_propagate(Quaternion_t* q, const double omega[3], double dt) { double wx = omega[0], wy = omega[1], wz = omega[2]; double q1=q->q1, q2=q->q2, q3=q->q3, q4=q->q4; // Omega matrix times quaternion / 2 double dq1 = 0.5 * ( q4*wx - q3*wy + q2*wz); double dq2 = 0.5 * ( q3*wx + q4*wy - q1*wz); double dq3 = 0.5 * (-q2*wx + q1*wy + q4*wz); double dq4 = 0.5 * (-q1*wx - q2*wy - q3*wz); q->q1 += dq1 * dt; q->q2 += dq2 * dt; q->q3 += dq3 * dt; q->q4 += dq4 * dt; quat_normalize(q); // renormalize after integration }

searchDocs.title: Convention Warning​
searchDocs.content: There is no universal quaternion convention. Different textbooks, software packages, and sensor vendors use different orderings:  Convention	Order	Used ByHamilton	[qx,qy,qz,qw][q_x, q_y, q_z, q_w][qx​,qy​,qz​,qw​]	Most aerospace textbooks, MATLAB JPL	[qx,qy,qz,qw][q_x, q_y, q_z, q_w][qx​,qy​,qz​,qw​] with different rotation direction	NASA JPL, many spacecraft Shuster (passive)	[q1,q2,q3,q4][q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4][q1​,q2​,q3​,q4​]	TRIAD, QUEST algorithms  The difference matters: a quaternion from one convention used directly in code for another convention produces the wrong rotation — often exactly opposite (180° error).  Rule: Define your convention explicitly, document it at the top of every file that uses quaternions, and have an explicit conversion when interfacing with external libraries or sensor datasheets.

searchDocs.title: The Particle Environment​
searchDocs.content: Three primary sources:  Van Allen Radiation BeltsTrapped protons and electrons orbiting Earth. Two distinct belts: inner belt (protons, ~1,000–6,000 km altitude) and outer belt (electrons, ~13,000–60,000 km). LEO sits inside or below the belts but still experiences significant flux, especially at high inclinations (polar orbit passes through the South Atlantic Anomaly).  Galactic Cosmic Rays (GCR)High-energy heavy ions from outside the solar system. Constant flux, low particle rate but very high energy per particle. The most damaging single-event effect source. Cannot be shielded cost-effectively — even thick aluminum only increases dose by slowing protons while generating secondary X-rays.  Solar Energetic Particles (SEP)Protons and heavy ions ejected during solar flares and coronal mass ejections. Highly variable — a major SEP event can increase radiation dose by 2–3 orders of magnitude for hours to days. Most mission designers plan for a worst-case SEP event occurring once per mission lifetime.

searchDocs.title: Single Event Upset (SEU)​
searchDocs.content: A high-energy particle passes through a memory cell, deposits charge, and flips the stored bit. The cell itself is not damaged — the flip is "soft" and a rewrite restores correct operation.  Rate: In LEO at moderate inclination, a modern 32-bit processor experiences roughly 10–100 SEUs per day in unprotected SRAM.  Software impact:  A variable storing a spacecraft mode could flip from NOMINAL to SAFE_MODE (or vice versa)A control law coefficient could change signA pointer could point to the wrong memory locationA jump table entry could redirect execution to arbitrary code  Mitigation:  ECC (Error Correcting Code) memory — hardware detects and corrects 1-bit errors, detects 2-bit errorsMemory scrubbing — periodically read and rewrite all memory to correct soft errors before they accumulateCritical variable redundancy — store important values in triplicate

searchDocs.title: Single Event Latchup (SEL)​
searchDocs.content: A particle triggers a parasitic PNPN thyristor structure in CMOS silicon. Once latched, the device draws excessive current and will burn out unless power is cycled within milliseconds.  Rate: Less common than SEU, but far more serious. Some devices are inherently latch-up immune (SOI process, BiCMOS) — rad-hard parts are designed to eliminate the parasitic structure.  Software impact:  The affected device stops respondingThe board may power off unexpectedlyIf the OBC itself latches up, the spacecraft loses all software execution  Mitigation:  Use radiation-hardened (rad-hard) or radiation-tolerant components — latch-up immune by designHardware current monitors that can cut power and cycle it back (latch-up protection circuit)Watchdog timers — if the OBC locks up, the watchdog fires and resets the system  // Latchup recovery in power management firmware void check_subsystem_current(void) { for (int i = 0; i < NUM_SUBSYSTEMS; i++) { float current_mA = read_current_sensor(i); if (current_mA > subsystem_limits[i].latchup_threshold_mA) { // Probable latchup — cut power and wait log_event(EVENT_LATCHUP_DETECTED, i, (uint32_t)current_mA); power_subsystem_off(i); delay_ms(500); // allow latch to release power_subsystem_on(i); // restore power subsystem_latchup_count[i]++; // If repeated latchups, leave subsystem off and alert FDIR if (subsystem_latchup_count[i] > MAX_LATCHUP_RETRIES) { power_subsystem_off(i); fdir_report_anomaly(ANOMALY_PERSISTENT_LATCHUP, i); } } } }

searchDocs.title: Single Event Functional Interrupt (SEFi)​
searchDocs.content: A particle corrupts a configuration register, mode register, or internal state machine of a complex device (FPGA, processor, memory controller). The device enters an unexpected state and may require a reset to recover.  Common in FPGAs — configuration memory is especially vulnerable. See FPGAs in Space for FPGA-specific mitigation.

searchDocs.title: Single Event Transient (SET)​
searchDocs.content: A particle causes a voltage glitch in combinational logic (not a storage element). In sequential circuits, the glitch may be latched on a clock edge, causing a logic error. In analog circuits, it causes a transient on a signal line.

searchDocs.title: Total Ionizing Dose (TID)​
searchDocs.content: Cumulative damage from ionizing radiation absorbed over the mission lifetime. Unlike SEEs, TID is a gradual degradation of device characteristics.  Effects:  Threshold voltage shift in MOSFETs — can cause logic cells to fail to switchIncreased leakage current — raises power consumption and heatGain degradation in bipolar transistorsOxide charging in gate oxides  Measurement: krad(Si) — killorads absorbed in silicon equivalent  Typical values:  Orbit	Annual Dose	5-Year MissionLEO (500 km, 28°)	0.5–2 krad/yr	2.5–10 krad LEO (550 km, 98° polar)	3–10 krad/yr	15–50 krad GEO (35,786 km)	5–20 krad/yr	25–100 krad Interplanetary	Variable, can be very high	Mission dependent  Software implication: TID doesn't directly affect software, but it degrades hardware reliability over time. Flight software should log total-dose estimates and flag components that may be approaching TID limits.

searchDocs.title: The SEU Rate Calculation​
searchDocs.content: For a memory array:  SEU rate=σLET×Φ\text{SEU rate} = \sigma_{LET} \times \PhiSEU rate=σLET​×Φ  Where σLET\sigma_{LET}σLET​ is the device cross-section (cm²/bit) at a given LET threshold, and Φ\PhiΦ is the particle flux (particles/cm²/s).  For COTS SRAM at 65 nm technology in LEO:  Cross-section: ~10⁻¹⁴ cm²/bit typicalFlux at 550 km: ~10⁴ particles/cm²/s (trapped protons + GCR)1 MB = 8 × 10⁶ bitsRate: ≈ 0.8 upsets/day  This is why scrubbing is necessary — at this rate, an unprotected 1 MB memory accumulates multiple soft errors per day.

searchDocs.title: Practical Summary for Software Engineers​
searchDocs.content: Threat	Rate	What Breaks	Your DefenseSEU	10–100/day (unprotected)	Bits in RAM, registers	ECC, scrubbing, redundancy SEL	Rare but possible	Power consumption, device death	Rad-hard parts, current monitors, WDT SEFi	Occasional (FPGAs)	FPGA configuration	Periodic reconfiguration, scrubbing TID	Cumulative	Device parametrics over years	Rad-hard parts, design margins  The baseline requirement for any spacecraft processor: ECC-protected RAM, a hardware watchdog, and a memory scrubbing task.

searchDocs.title: Why Scrubbing Is Necessary​
searchDocs.content: SRAM protected by ECC can correct single-bit errors and detect (but not correct) double-bit errors. But ECC only acts when memory is read. If a bit flips and is never read, another bit in the same word may flip before the first error is noticed. Now you have a double-bit error — detected but uncorrectable.  Without scrubbing:  Bit A flips in address 0x20001000 (SEU) — ECC notes 1-bit error on next read3 days later, bit B flips in the same word — now a 2-bit errorNext read: ECC detects double-bit error → generates fault → triggers safe mode  With scrubbing:  Bit A flips (SEU)Scrubber reads 0x20001000 within 10 minutes — ECC corrects bit A, writes back corrected valueMemory is clean again  The scrub interval determines your protection window. For LEO at 10–100 SEUs/day in 1 MB of SRAM, one scrub every few minutes is typically sufficient.

searchDocs.title: ECC Hardware Interface​
searchDocs.content: On most spacecraft processors (RAD750, LEON3, ARM Cortex-R with ECC), the ECC logic is transparent to the software for corrections — the hardware reads, checks, corrects, and writes back automatically on a memory access.  The software interface is through status/error registers:  // Example: LEON3 EDAC controller register map #define EDAC_BASE 0x80000200UL #define EDAC_STATUS_REG (*(volatile uint32_t*)(EDAC_BASE + 0x00)) #define EDAC_FAILING_ADDR (*(volatile uint32_t*)(EDAC_BASE + 0x04)) #define EDAC_ERROR_COUNT (*(volatile uint32_t*)(EDAC_BASE + 0x08)) // Status register bits #define EDAC_CE_FLAG (1U << 0) // correctable error occurred #define EDAC_UE_FLAG (1U << 1) // uncorrectable error occurred #define EDAC_CE_CLEAR (1U << 8) // write 1 to clear CE flag typedef struct { uint32_t ce_count; // correctable errors detected uint32_t ue_count; // uncorrectable errors uint32_t last_ce_address; // address of last corrected error uint32_t last_ue_address; // address of last uncorrected error } EDAC_Stats_t; static EDAC_Stats_t edac_stats = {0}; // Check and clear EDAC status — called after scrub operations void edac_check_status(uint32_t scrubbed_address) { uint32_t status = EDAC_STATUS_REG; if (status & EDAC_CE_FLAG) { edac_stats.ce_count++; edac_stats.last_ce_address = EDAC_FAILING_ADDR; // Log correctable error — important for trend analysis log_event(EVENT_ECC_CORRECTED, edac_stats.last_ce_address, edac_stats.ce_count); // Clear the flag EDAC_STATUS_REG = EDAC_CE_CLEAR; } if (status & EDAC_UE_FLAG) { edac_stats.ue_count++; edac_stats.last_ue_address = EDAC_FAILING_ADDR; // Uncorrectable error — this is serious log_event(EVENT_ECC_UNCORRECTABLE, edac_stats.last_ue_address, edac_stats.ue_count); // Report to fault management fdir_report_anomaly(ANOMALY_ECC_UNCORRECTABLE, edac_stats.last_ue_address); } }

searchDocs.title: The Scrubber Task​
searchDocs.content: // Memory regions to scrub — defined at link time extern uint32_t __sram_start; extern uint32_t __sram_end; extern uint32_t __sram_critical_start; extern uint32_t __sram_critical_end; #define SCRUB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES 4096U // scrub 4 KB per iteration #define SCRUB_INTERVAL_MS 100U // yield every 100 ms typedef struct { uint32_t* current_addr; // current scrub position uint32_t* region_start; uint32_t* region_end; uint32_t scrubs_completed; // full-pass counter uint32_t ce_total; // lifetime CE count } Scrubber_State_t; static Scrubber_State_t scrubber = {0}; void scrubber_init(void) { scrubber.region_start = &__sram_start; scrubber.region_end = &__sram_end; scrubber.current_addr = scrubber.region_start; scrubber.scrubs_completed = 0; } // Scrub one chunk — call from task, yields between chunks void scrubber_run_chunk(void) { uint32_t* end_of_chunk = scrubber.current_addr + (SCRUB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES / sizeof(uint32_t)); if (end_of_chunk > scrubber.region_end) { end_of_chunk = scrubber.region_end; } // Read each word — ECC hardware auto-corrects and logs errors // The read itself triggers ECC correction; we also check status volatile uint32_t* p = (volatile uint32_t*)scrubber.current_addr; while (p < (volatile uint32_t*)end_of_chunk) { volatile uint32_t val = *p; // triggers ECC check (void)val; // suppress unused-variable warning p++; } // Check if ECC detected anything during this chunk edac_check_status((uint32_t)scrubber.current_addr); // Advance position scrubber.current_addr = (uint32_t*)end_of_chunk; // Wrap around — completed one full pass if (scrubber.current_addr >= scrubber.region_end) { scrubber.current_addr = scrubber.region_start; scrubber.scrubs_completed++; log_event(EVENT_SCRUB_PASS_COMPLETE, scrubber.scrubs_completed, edac_stats.ce_count); } } // RTOS task — runs continuously in background void vScrubberTask(void* pvParams) { scrubber_init(); for (;;) { scrubber_run_chunk(); // Yield — let higher-priority tasks run // 100 ms between chunks: 4 KB * (total_RAM / 4096) = scrub time // For 1 MB SRAM: 256 chunks * 100 ms = ~25 seconds per full pass vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(SCRUB_INTERVAL_MS)); } }

searchDocs.title: Critical Memory Priority Scrubbing​
searchDocs.content: Not all memory is equal. Code in SRAM, active flight software state, and attitude control data are more critical than buffered telemetry. A scrubber can prioritize:  // Two-region scrubber: critical memory scrubbed 10x more frequently #define CRITICAL_SCRUB_RATIO 10 // scrub critical region 10x per general region pass static uint32_t critical_scrub_counter = 0; void scrubber_run_prioritized(void) { // Always scrub critical region scrub_region(&__sram_critical_start, &__sram_critical_end, SCRUB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES); // Only scrub general region 1 in N cycles critical_scrub_counter++; if (critical_scrub_counter >= CRITICAL_SCRUB_RATIO) { critical_scrub_counter = 0; scrub_region(&__sram_start, &__sram_end, SCRUB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES); } }

searchDocs.title: Scrub Rate Analysis​
searchDocs.content: How often must you scrub to keep uncorrectable error probability below mission requirements?  Given:  Single-event upset rate: λ=50\lambda = 50λ=50 upsets/day in 1 MB SRAM = 5.8×10−45.8 \times 10^{-4}5.8×10−4 upsets/secondWord size: 32 bits (4 bytes)Words in 1 MB: 262,144 wordsPer-word SEU rate: λw=λ/262144=2.2×10−9\lambda_w = \lambda / 262144 = 2.2 \times 10^{-9}λw​=λ/262144=2.2×10−9 upsets/second/word  Probability of a second upset in the same word before scrub interval TsT_sTs​:  PUE≈λw⋅Ts⋅λw⋅Ts/2⋅NwordsP_{UE} \approx \lambda_w \cdot T_s \cdot \lambda_w \cdot T_s / 2 \cdot N_{words}PUE​≈λw​⋅Ts​⋅λw​⋅Ts​/2⋅Nwords​  For Ts=1800T_s = 1800Ts​=1800 s (30 min) and 262,144 words:  PUE≈(2.2×10−9)2×(1800)2/2×262144≈2.3×10−6 per dayP_{UE} \approx (2.2 \times 10^{-9})^2 \times (1800)^2 / 2 \times 262144 \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per day}PUE​≈(2.2×10−9)2×(1800)2/2×262144≈2.3×10−6 per day  Acceptable for most missions. Tightening TsT_sTs​ to 300 s (5 min) reduces this by 36×36\times36×.

searchDocs.title: Integration with Fault Management​
searchDocs.content: The scrubber should report to the spacecraft's fault management system:  // Thresholds — tuned for mission radiation environment #define CE_RATE_WARNING_PER_HOUR 10 // investigate if exceeded #define CE_RATE_CRITICAL_PER_HOUR 50 // trigger safe mode evaluation #define UE_COUNT_SAFE_MODE_TRIGGER 3 // 3 UEs → mandatory safe mode // Called hourly by health monitor void scrubber_health_check(void) { float ce_rate = (float)(edac_stats.ce_count - edac_stats.ce_count_last_hour) / 1.0f; edac_stats.ce_count_last_hour = edac_stats.ce_count; if (ce_rate > CE_RATE_CRITICAL_PER_HOUR) { fdir_report_anomaly(ANOMALY_HIGH_CE_RATE, (uint32_t)ce_rate); } if (edac_stats.ue_count >= UE_COUNT_SAFE_MODE_TRIGGER) { fdir_report_anomaly(ANOMALY_UE_THRESHOLD_EXCEEDED, edac_stats.ue_count); } }   A rising correctable error rate is an early indicator of increasing radiation environment (approaching SAA, solar event) or hardware degradation (TID). Logging it allows mission operators to respond proactively.

searchDocs.title: The Principle​
searchDocs.content: Input → Compute A ──┐ Input → Compute B ──┼──→ Voter → Output (majority) Input → Compute C ──┘ If A=1, B=1, C=0 → output 1 (A and B agree) If A=1, B=0, C=0 → output 0 (B and C agree) If A=1, B=1, C=1 → output 1 (all agree)   TMR tolerates any single fault. It does not tolerate two simultaneous faults affecting different channels — for that you need quadruple redundancy or other techniques.

searchDocs.title: Integer Voter​
searchDocs.content: #include <stdint.h> #include <stdbool.h> typedef enum { VOTE_RESULT_UNANIMOUS, // all three agree VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY, // two agree, one disagrees (fault detected) VOTE_RESULT_FAILED, // all three disagree (cannot determine correct value) } VoteResult_t; // Vote on three 32-bit integer values // Returns the majority value, result indicates agreement level uint32_t tmr_vote_u32(uint32_t a, uint32_t b, uint32_t c, VoteResult_t* result) { if (a == b && b == c) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_UNANIMOUS; return a; } else if (a == b) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY; // c is the outlier return a; } else if (a == c) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY; // b is the outlier return a; } else if (b == c) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY; // a is the outlier return b; } else { *result = VOTE_RESULT_FAILED; return a; // arbitrary — caller must handle FAILED case } }

searchDocs.title: Floating-Point Voter with Tolerance​
searchDocs.content: For floating-point values (sensor readings, computed states), exact equality is unreliable due to rounding. Use a tolerance-based voter:  #include <math.h> #include <float.h> // Vote on three double values within a tolerance // Returns the median (robust to one outlier) and reports divergence double tmr_vote_double(double a, double b, double c, double tolerance, VoteResult_t* result) { double diff_ab = fabs(a - b); double diff_ac = fabs(a - c); double diff_bc = fabs(b - c); bool ab_agree = (diff_ab <= tolerance); bool ac_agree = (diff_ac <= tolerance); bool bc_agree = (diff_bc <= tolerance); if (ab_agree && ac_agree && bc_agree) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_UNANIMOUS; return (a + b + c) / 3.0; // average for minimum noise } else if (ab_agree) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY; // c is outlier return (a + b) / 2.0; } else if (ac_agree) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY; // b is outlier return (a + c) / 2.0; } else if (bc_agree) { *result = VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY; // a is outlier return (b + c) / 2.0; } else { *result = VOTE_RESULT_FAILED; // All three disagree — return median as best guess double sorted[3] = {a, b, c}; // Simple 3-element sort if (sorted[0] > sorted[1]) { double t=sorted[0]; sorted[0]=sorted[1]; sorted[1]=t; } if (sorted[1] > sorted[2]) { double t=sorted[1]; sorted[1]=sorted[2]; sorted[2]=t; } if (sorted[0] > sorted[1]) { double t=sorted[0]; sorted[0]=sorted[1]; sorted[1]=t; } return sorted[1]; // median } }

searchDocs.title: TMR-Protected Critical Variable​
searchDocs.content: For variables so critical that a single SEU must not corrupt them — spacecraft mode, thruster enable flags, communication encryption keys:  // TMR-protected variable — stored in three separate memory locations // Linker can be configured to place these in different SRAM banks typedef struct { uint32_t copy_a; uint32_t padding_a[15]; // pad to separate cache lines / memory regions uint32_t copy_b; uint32_t padding_b[15]; uint32_t copy_c; uint32_t padding_c[15]; } TMR_Uint32_t; void tmr_write(TMR_Uint32_t* tmr, uint32_t value) { tmr->copy_a = value; tmr->copy_b = value; tmr->copy_c = value; } uint32_t tmr_read(TMR_Uint32_t* tmr, bool* fault_detected) { VoteResult_t result; uint32_t value = tmr_vote_u32(tmr->copy_a, tmr->copy_b, tmr->copy_c, &result); if (result == VOTE_RESULT_MAJORITY) { // One copy was corrupted — correct all three copies tmr_write(tmr, value); *fault_detected = true; log_event(EVENT_TMR_CORRECTION, (uint32_t)tmr, 0); } else if (result == VOTE_RESULT_FAILED) { // Severe corruption — all three disagree // Best we can do: use majority of bits across the three values *fault_detected = true; fdir_report_anomaly(ANOMALY_TMR_FAILURE, (uint32_t)tmr); } else { *fault_detected = false; } return value; } // Usage: spacecraft mode register static TMR_Uint32_t g_spacecraft_mode; void set_spacecraft_mode(SpacecraftMode_t mode) { tmr_write(&g_spacecraft_mode, (uint32_t)mode); } SpacecraftMode_t get_spacecraft_mode(void) { bool fault; uint32_t mode = tmr_read(&g_spacecraft_mode, &fault); return (SpacecraftMode_t)mode; }

searchDocs.title: TMR for Complete Function Execution​
searchDocs.content: On processors with lockstep support (ARM Cortex-R with lockstep, LEON3 with FT mode), TMR can be applied at the hardware level — two or three CPU cores execute the same instructions and results are compared cycle by cycle. A discrepancy triggers a fault.  In software, you can implement TMR for function outputs:  // Run a critical function three times and vote on the result // Used for computations where a transient SEU during execution could corrupt output double compute_control_torque_tmr(const ControlInput_t* input) { // Run three independent computations double result_a = compute_control_torque(input); double result_b = compute_control_torque(input); double result_c = compute_control_torque(input); VoteResult_t vote; double voted = tmr_vote_double(result_a, result_b, result_c, TORQUE_TOLERANCE_NM, &vote); if (vote != VOTE_RESULT_UNANIMOUS) { log_event(EVENT_TMR_SOFT_FAULT, 0, 0); } return voted; }   This is expensive — 3x compute cost — and only justified for functions where a corrupted output has catastrophic consequences (thruster firing commands, safe mode transitions).

searchDocs.title: Where TMR Is Applied in Practice​
searchDocs.content: Application	Technique	WhySpacecraft mode register	Software TMR variable	Mode corrupted by SEU → wrong behavior FPGA configuration	Redundant configuration frames + scrubbing	SEU in FPGA config → functional failure Processor core (Cortex-R, LEON-FT)	Hardware lockstep	Transient execution error in CPU Critical command validation	Triple-check before execution	Ensure thruster/pyro commands are intentional Memory-mapped safety outputs	TMR registers in FPGA	Prevent single-event from triggering pyros

searchDocs.title: The Loop Structure​
searchDocs.content: Sensor Data Estimation Control Actuators ────────── ────────── ─────── ───────── IMU (gyro) → → Reaction Wheels Magnetometer → Extended → PD → Magnetorquers Sun Sensor → Kalman Filter → Controller → Thrusters Star Tracker → (if available)   Rate: 10 Hz — one complete cycle every 100 ms. This is the hard deadline.

searchDocs.title: Data Structures​
searchDocs.content: // Quaternion: [q1 q2 q3 | q4] = [vector | scalar] // Convention: body frame to ECI frame typedef struct { double q1, q2, q3; // vector part double q4; // scalar part (cos(theta/2)) } Quaternion_t; // Angular rate in body frame (rad/s) typedef struct { double x, y, z; } AngularRate_t; // Sensor package — filled by sensor drivers each cycle typedef struct { AngularRate_t gyro; // body angular rate (rad/s) double mag_body[3]; // magnetic field in body frame (nT) double sun_body[3]; // sun direction in body frame (unit vector) bool mag_valid; bool sun_valid; uint64_t timestamp_us; // microseconds since mission epoch } SensorMeasurement_t; // EKF state typedef struct { Quaternion_t q_est; // estimated attitude (body→ECI) double gyro_bias[3]; // estimated gyro bias (rad/s) double P[6][6]; // error covariance matrix } EKF_State_t; // Control torque command typedef struct { double torque_Nm[3]; // commanded torque in body frame } TorqueCommand_t; // Reaction wheel speeds typedef struct { double speed_rads[4]; // 4-wheel pyramid configuration } WheelSpeeds_t;

searchDocs.title: Sensor Reading​
searchDocs.content: // Called at start of each 100 ms cycle // Each driver is interrupt-driven — data is ready in a buffer bool adcs_read_sensors(SensorMeasurement_t* meas) { meas->timestamp_us = get_mission_time_us(); // IMU (gyroscope + accelerometer) via SPI // IMU runs at 100 Hz; data is in double buffer, this reads latest if (!imu_read_gyro(&meas->gyro)) { log_event(EVENT_IMU_READ_FAIL, 0, 0); return false; // IMU failure — cannot run ADCS without gyro } // Magnetometer via I2C double mag_raw[3]; meas->mag_valid = magnetometer_read(mag_raw); if (meas->mag_valid) { // Apply calibration matrix (pre-loaded from TBL table) calibrate_magnetometer(mag_raw, meas->mag_body); } // Sun sensor via analog ADC meas->sun_valid = sun_sensor_read(meas->sun_body); return true; }

searchDocs.title: Attitude Estimator (Extended Kalman Filter)​
searchDocs.content: The EKF fuses gyroscope data (high rate, drifts) with absolute reference measurements from magnetometer and sun sensor (lower rate, no drift).  State vector: x=[δθ,b]T\mathbf{x} = [\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{b}]^Tx=[δθ,b]T — attitude error (3) + gyro bias (3).  // EKF state is persistent across cycles static EKF_State_t ekf; void ekf_init(const Quaternion_t* q_initial) { ekf.q_est = *q_initial; ekf.gyro_bias[0] = ekf.gyro_bias[1] = ekf.gyro_bias[2] = 0.0; // Initial covariance — how uncertain are we? memset(ekf.P, 0, sizeof(ekf.P)); // Attitude uncertainty: 1 degree = 0.0175 rad ekf.P[0][0] = ekf.P[1][1] = ekf.P[2][2] = 0.0175 * 0.0175; // Gyro bias uncertainty: 0.01 deg/s = 1.7e-4 rad/s ekf.P[3][3] = ekf.P[4][4] = ekf.P[5][5] = 1.7e-4 * 1.7e-4; } // Propagation step — run every cycle (10 Hz) static void ekf_propagate(const AngularRate_t* gyro, double dt) { // Correct gyro measurement for estimated bias double omega[3]; omega[0] = gyro->x - ekf.gyro_bias[0]; omega[1] = gyro->y - ekf.gyro_bias[1]; omega[2] = gyro->z - ekf.gyro_bias[2]; // Propagate quaternion quat_propagate(&ekf.q_est, omega, dt); // Propagate covariance: P = F*P*F' + Q // (simplified — full implementation uses matrix math library) double sigma_gyro = 1e-5; // gyro noise (rad/s/sqrt(Hz)) double sigma_bias = 1e-7; // bias drift noise for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { ekf.P[i][i] += sigma_gyro * sigma_gyro * dt; ekf.P[i+3][i+3] += sigma_bias * sigma_bias * dt; } } // Update step — run when valid reference measurement available static void ekf_update_magnetometer(const double* mag_body, const double* mag_ref_eci) { // Predict where magnetometer should point given current attitude estimate double mag_predicted[3]; // Rotate reference field (ECI) to body frame using estimated attitude Quaternion_t q_inv = quat_conjugate(&ekf.q_est); quat_rotate_vector(&q_inv, mag_ref_eci, mag_predicted); // Innovation: difference between measured and predicted double innov[3]; innov[0] = mag_body[0] - mag_predicted[0]; innov[1] = mag_body[1] - mag_predicted[1]; innov[2] = mag_body[2] - mag_predicted[2]; // Innovation covariance and Kalman gain computation // (full implementation here for production code) double sigma_mag = 100.0; // magnetometer noise (nT) double K[3]; // simplified scalar gain per axis for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { double S = ekf.P[i][i] + sigma_mag * sigma_mag; K[i] = ekf.P[i][i] / S; // Update attitude error estimate double delta_theta = K[i] * innov[i]; // Apply correction to quaternion (small angle approximation) // [full rotation vector correction in production] ekf.P[i][i] *= (1.0 - K[i]); // update covariance } quat_normalize(&ekf.q_est); }

searchDocs.title: PD Attitude Controller​
searchDocs.content: Given estimated attitude and a target attitude, compute the torque to apply.  τ=−kp⋅δθ−kd⋅ω\boldsymbol{\tau} = -k_p \cdot \delta\boldsymbol{\theta} - k_d \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega}τ=−kp​⋅δθ−kd​⋅ω  Where δθ\delta\boldsymbol{\theta}δθ is the attitude error (axis-angle) and ω\boldsymbol{\omega}ω is the angular rate.  typedef struct { double kp; // proportional gain (Nm/rad) double kd; // derivative gain (Nm·s/rad) double max_torque_Nm; // saturation limit } PDGains_t; // Gains loaded from TBL table — can be updated by ground command static PDGains_t gains; TorqueCommand_t adcs_compute_control( const Quaternion_t* q_est, // estimated attitude const Quaternion_t* q_target, // desired attitude const AngularRate_t* omega, // current angular rate const PDGains_t* g) { TorqueCommand_t cmd = {0}; // Attitude error quaternion: q_err = q_target_conj * q_est Quaternion_t q_t_conj = quat_conjugate(q_target); Quaternion_t q_err = quat_multiply(&q_t_conj, q_est); // If scalar part is negative, flip to take short path if (q_err.q4 < 0.0) { q_err.q1 = -q_err.q1; q_err.q2 = -q_err.q2; q_err.q3 = -q_err.q3; q_err.q4 = -q_err.q4; } // Error angle (approximately 2 * vector part for small angles) double err[3] = {2.0 * q_err.q1, 2.0 * q_err.q2, 2.0 * q_err.q3}; // PD control law cmd.torque_Nm[0] = -g->kp * err[0] - g->kd * omega->x; cmd.torque_Nm[1] = -g->kp * err[1] - g->kd * omega->y; cmd.torque_Nm[2] = -g->kp * err[2] - g->kd * omega->z; // Saturate torque commands for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { if (cmd.torque_Nm[i] > g->max_torque_Nm) cmd.torque_Nm[i] = g->max_torque_Nm; if (cmd.torque_Nm[i] < -g->max_torque_Nm) cmd.torque_Nm[i] = -g->max_torque_Nm; } return cmd; }

searchDocs.title: Reaction Wheel Torque Distribution​
searchDocs.content: A spacecraft typically has 4 reaction wheels in a pyramid configuration. The torque commands must be distributed across the 4 wheels:  // Torque distribution matrix (pseudo-inverse of wheel geometry matrix) // For a pyramid configuration with 54.7° tilt angle static const double A_inv[4][3] = { { 0.577, -0.577, 0.289 }, { -0.577, -0.577, 0.289 }, { -0.577, 0.577, 0.289 }, { 0.577, 0.577, 0.289 }, }; WheelSpeeds_t adcs_torque_to_wheel_speeds( const TorqueCommand_t* cmd, const WheelSpeeds_t* current_speeds, double dt, double wheel_inertia_kgm2) { WheelSpeeds_t new_speeds; double delta_h[4]; // change in angular momentum for each wheel // delta_H = A_inv * torque * dt for (int w = 0; w < 4; w++) { delta_h[w] = 0.0; for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) { delta_h[w] += A_inv[w][i] * cmd->torque_Nm[i] * dt; } // delta_omega = delta_H / I_wheel new_speeds.speed_rads[w] = current_speeds->speed_rads[w] + delta_h[w] / wheel_inertia_kgm2; // Clamp to maximum wheel speed const double MAX_SPEED = 628.3; // 6000 RPM in rad/s if (new_speeds.speed_rads[w] > MAX_SPEED) new_speeds.speed_rads[w] = MAX_SPEED; if (new_speeds.speed_rads[w] < -MAX_SPEED) new_speeds.speed_rads[w] = -MAX_SPEED; } return new_speeds; }

searchDocs.title: The Full RTOS Task​
searchDocs.content: // ADCS task — runs at 10 Hz, highest priority in FSW void vADCS_ControlTask(void* pvParams) { TickType_t xLastWakeTime = xTaskGetTickCount(); const TickType_t xPeriod = pdMS_TO_TICKS(100); // 100 ms = 10 Hz SensorMeasurement_t sensors; WheelSpeeds_t current_wheel_speeds = {0}; Quaternion_t q_target = QUAT_IDENTITY; // nadir-pointing target // Initialize EKF with coarse initial attitude from sun sensor Quaternion_t q_init = get_initial_attitude_estimate(); ekf_init(&q_init); for (;;) { // 1. Read sensors bool sensor_ok = adcs_read_sensors(&sensors); if (!sensor_ok) { // Sensor failure — report to FDIR, continue with last known state fdir_report_anomaly(ANOMALY_IMU_FAILURE, 0); } else { // 2. EKF propagation (always runs if we have gyro) ekf_propagate(&sensors.gyro, 0.1); // dt = 100 ms // 3. EKF measurement update (when absolute reference available) if (sensors.mag_valid) { double mag_ref[3]; igrf_compute_model(get_mission_time_s(), mag_ref); // WMM/IGRF model ekf_update_magnetometer(sensors.mag_body, mag_ref); } // 4. Get current attitude target from mode manager q_target = mode_manager_get_attitude_target(); // 5. Compute control torque TorqueCommand_t torque_cmd = adcs_compute_control( &ekf.q_est, &q_target, &sensors.gyro, &gains); // 6. Distribute to reaction wheels WheelSpeeds_t cmd_speeds = adcs_torque_to_wheel_speeds( &torque_cmd, &current_wheel_speeds, 0.1, WHEEL_INERTIA); // 7. Command actuators reaction_wheel_command(&cmd_speeds); current_wheel_speeds = cmd_speeds; // 8. Publish telemetry adcs_publish_telemetry(&ekf.q_est, &sensors.gyro, &torque_cmd); } // Block until next 100 ms cycle — maintains exact periodicity vTaskDelayUntil(&xLastWakeTime, xPeriod); } }   This is the complete ADCS loop. Every function call in this task connects to a topic in this reference:  Sensor reading → Sensors & ActuatorsEKF → Kalman FilterQuaternion operations → Attitude RepresentationRTOS task timing → FreeRTOS, SchedulingFDIR reporting → Fault ManagementTelemetry → Telemetry Pipeline
